report.executive_summary:
PR #1231 adds an optional, greenfield `k8s/` subtree that deploys NeMo Gym on Kubernetes/OpenShift via KubeRay, plus two CI workflows (image build + kind e2e). It is a thoughtful, security-conscious external contribution: full pod-level hardening (runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, drop-ALL caps, seccomp RuntimeDefault), per-instance namePrefix+label isolation enabling multiple benchmarks in one namespace, an end-to-end kind smoke test, sensible graceful-shutdown rollout config, and arbitrary-UID/OpenShift support. Nothing here is a deploy-breaker, security hole, or data-loss bug; the happy path is exercised by CI and works.

After deduping the 38 input findings (F1–F32 plus C1–C8) and applying the adjudicator's verdicts, the substance reduces to a cluster of documentation-accuracy fixes and a few real engineering nits. Two documentation claims are materially false and should be corrected before merge: (1) the root README advertises "mTLS-authenticated distributed task execution" while zero TLS/mTLS is configured anywhere (all intra-cluster traffic is plaintext) — verified verbatim at README.md:311 with no TLS in k8s/; and (2) the README's "Pre-built images are available from GHCR" plus the verify-table "3/3" head-pod count are wrong/premature and will mislead early adopters. The only non-doc items worth acting on are CI hygiene (missing `concurrency` groups; helm operator unpinned vs README's 1.3.0), a coverage gap (cross-node Ray `.remote()` dispatch is never asserted by CI), and a probe/lifecycle coupling where the resources server's liveness probe bounces the whole Ray worker pod.

A large share of the original findings were downgraded or refuted on inspection: several cited line numbers from a NEWER base checkout than the PR branch (the `GET /` liveness endpoint did not exist when the PR was authored — F3), several "bugs" were defensible idiomatic choices (per-target gha cache scopes, gitignored secret.yaml, Burstable QoS, absent ServiceMonitor), and one (F24, bash on UBI10-minimal) was empirically refuted by pulling the image. Recommendation: approve with the documentation corrections and the cheap CI fixes; treat everything else as optional polish or follow-up.

confirmed_findings.title: README claims "mTLS-authenticated distributed task execution" but no TLS/mTLS is configured anywhere
confirmed_findings.problem: README.md:311 states the resources server is "embedded in the RayCluster for mTLS-authenticated distributed task execution." A scoped grep over k8s/ for mtls|tls|cert|RAY_USE_TLS|RAY_TLS_|mesh|istio|spiffe|networkpolicy returns ZERO matches (re-verified). KubeRay does not enable inter-node TLS by default; the RayCluster env blocks (head, gym-workers, gym-resources) set only RAY_TMPDIR/NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES/NEMO_GYM_*/*_HOST — no RAY_USE_TLS=1 and no cert mounts. Inter-service HTTP is also plaintext (server_utils.py builds f"http://..." URLs; probes hit plaintext /docs). So all Ray GCS/raylet/gRPC and all agent/model/resources/head HTTP traffic is unencrypted and unauthenticated, and the README claim is false.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: This is a security-sensitive, user-facing claim. An operator could be given a false sense of transport encryption for a worker group that executes untrusted benchmark/code-gen code. The defect itself is a single false adjective (no runtime/exploit impact — the plaintext posture exists independent of the sentence, and the actual k8s/ deployment guide does not repeat the claim and correctly notes port-forward-only access), which is why it is medium rather than high.
confirmed_findings.fix: Remove "mTLS-authenticated" from README.md:311 (e.g. "...embedded in the RayCluster for distributed task execution") and accurately state that intra-cluster traffic is plaintext HTTP/gRPC with isolation scoped to namespace + label selectors and no external exposure. If encrypted transport is actually intended, implement it (RAY_USE_TLS=1 + RAY_TLS_SERVER_CERT/KEY/CA mounted on every RayCluster container, TLS termination or a mesh sidecar on the FastAPI services) rather than asserting it.

confirmed_findings.title: HeadServer is config-only in-cluster; README overstates that ng_status works against the cluster
confirmed_findings.problem: The standalone k8s/head/entrypoint.py builds HeadServer(config) and serves it directly but never calls set_server_instances() (only invoked on the local RunHelper.start path). So /server_instances permanently returns [], and ng_status against the cluster always prints "No NeMo Gym servers found running." Yet k8s/README.md:145/343 lists ng_status among tools the HeadServer "enables ... to work against the cluster." The description also miscited OmegaConf.to_yaml(resolve=False) (the code has no resolve kwarg), and the README ng_collect_rollouts example's POLICY_* env exports map to OS env vars that are NOT in the config-resolution path (lowercase policy_* keys come from env.yaml/CLI), so they are effectively no-ops.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: A user following the docs runs ng_status, sees "no servers found," and concludes the deploy is broken when it is healthy. The HeadServer is genuinely functional (its /global_config_dict_yaml IS the path ng_collect_rollouts uses) — only the ng_status path and the doc framing are wrong.
confirmed_findings.fix: In k8s/README.md, remove ng_status from the tools the HeadServer enables (or note it will report no instances because the standalone HeadServer does not register them). Document the HeadServer as config-only in-cluster (not vestigial). Re-examine/remove the no-op POLICY_* exports in the ng_collect_rollouts example, state the unstated prerequisite that these CLI commands run on a workstation with a local clone + uv sync + the benchmark's local data/config files, and verify ng_reward_profile's cluster path before listing it.

confirmed_findings.title: Base ships :latest + imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent on every workload; README's "images available" claim is premature
confirmed_findings.problem: Every base workload pins :latest with imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent and no overlay sets an images: transformer, so a node caching an older :latest silently runs stale code and Deployments do not roll on a re-push (unchanged pod template). The build workflow only pushes on non-PR events and tags :latest only on the default branch, so :latest does not exist until merge to main — yet README.md:77 states unconditionally "Pre-built images are available from GitHub Container Registry," guaranteeing ImagePullBackOff for pre-merge/fork deployers. NOTE: the original title's "non-reproducible / no way to pin without hand-editing" framing was empirically REFUTED — kustomize images: with digest: cleanly overrides base at the overlay level, and semver/sha tags exist post-merge.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Unsafe defaults plus an affirmatively misleading docs claim that produces ImagePullBackOff for the most likely early adopters is a real DX/operational defect, though not a deploy-breaker for the documented merge-to-main flow.
confirmed_findings.fix: Add a README caveat that GHCR images publish only after merge to main (and that GHCR packages default to private until visibility is set), so pre-merge/fork deployers must build from source. Document that production should pin a version/digest and note IfNotPresent's no-rollout behavior. Add a digest: example to the README images:-override section (currently only newName/newTag is shown). Optionally pin Dockerfile base images by @sha256.

confirmed_findings.title: Cross-node Ray task dispatch is never exercised by CI (only the no-Ray overlay is tested)
confirmed_findings.problem: CI deploys only the example_single_tool_call overlay (which has no ray.remote calls). So a Ray-using resources server dispatching .remote() from the num_cpus=0 gym-resources node onto a gym-worker, and autoscaler scale-up, are not asserted by any CI step. NOTE: the original "Ray is never exercised" framing is overstated — the CI resources server DOES run initialize_ray() (NEMO_GYM_SKIP_RAY_INIT is absent from the resources worker; configmap sets ray_head_node_address: "auto") and joins the live cluster, and the novel resources-as-Ray-worker postStart launch IS validated by the reward==1 smoke test.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: The PR's central value (running a distributed Ray dispatch topology on k8s) has an untested slice. A regression in cross-node dispatch would not be caught. This is a coverage gap, not an asserted defect — the 0-CPU dispatch design and autoscaler RBAC were independently judged sound.
confirmed_findings.fix: Add a CI step that deploys a Ray-using overlay (e.g. code-gen, whose app.py issues check_correctness_remote.remote()) and asserts a task completes, proving dispatch onto gym-workers end-to-end. Drop the "assert the autoscaler scales 1→4" idea as infeasible on a single-node kind runner (gym-workers request cpu:2/mem:4Gi each); at most document autoscaler scale-up as unverified by CI, and consider minReplicas:0 for non-Ray overlays.

confirmed_findings.title: Resources-server liveness probe on :9080 restarts the entire Ray worker pod, not just the wedged gym child
confirmed_findings.problem: In the gym-resources worker group, PID 1 is the Ray worker (ray start --block); the NeMo Gym resources server is only a detached postStart background child on :9080. The livenessProbe targets /docs:9080 — the child — so when it fails (~150s) the kubelet restarts the whole container, killing the raylet. A crashed child has no in-place supervisor, so recovery is the heavyweight container bounce. The fix is cheaper than implied: server_utils.py's initialize_ray() connects to Ray as a CLIENT via ray.init(address=...), so the resources server need not BE a Ray worker.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Couples the lifecycle of the probed child to the raylet. A wedged-but-alive server bounces the Ray node instead of restarting the child in place. NOTE: harm is bounded — gym-resources runs num-cpus=0 (loses no scheduled compute), autoscaling tolerates worker churn, and the 150s threshold rules out transient stalls — which is why this is medium, not high.
confirmed_findings.fix: Decouple the server from the raylet. Preferred: run the resources server as its own Deployment (mirroring deployment-agent/model) and let it connect to Ray as a client via the existing initialize_ray() path, giving it its own PID 1 so liveness restarts only the server. Minimal: drop the livenessProbe on :9080 (keep readiness, which de-routes the dead endpoint without bouncing the raylet) and add an in-place supervisor for the postStart child. Add a comment noting the probe currently health-checks a detached background child.

confirmed_findings.title: New CI workflows have no concurrency cancellation and trigger on broad source paths
confirmed_findings.problem: Neither new workflow defines a concurrency group, so rapid pushes queue duplicate full runs (4-image matrix build + kind e2e) with no cancel-in-progress. Both fire on broad paths including resources_servers/** — the dominant PR activity — so essentially every benchmark PR pays for the kind run. NOTE: PRs build-only (no GHCR push) and use GHA layer caching, so the real cost is duplicate queued runs, not a from-scratch 4-image build each time.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Avoidable runner/wall-clock cost on every benchmark PR; diverges from the repo's dominant convention (unit-tests.yml and publish-fern-docs.yml use cancel-in-progress: true for PR-triggered runs). Cheap to fix.
confirmed_findings.fix: Add `concurrency: { group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.ref }}, cancel-in-progress: true }` to both workflows (matches the dominant PR-triggered convention). Do NOT narrow the e2e trigger to k8s/** only — nemo_gym/server_utils.py is baked into the images and on the critical path; instead consider dropping responses_api_agents/** and responses_api_models/** from the e2e path filter since the mock-model e2e exercises neither.

confirmed_findings.title: README verify-table says the Ray head pod is 3/3; it is actually 2/2
confirmed_findings.problem: k8s/README.md:128 tells users to expect `cg-gym-ray-head-* 3/3` (re-verified verbatim). The head group defines exactly one container (ray-head); with enableInTreeAutoscaling:true KubeRay injects exactly one autoscaler sidecar = 2 containers = 2/2 Ready. No overlay adds a third container (the openshift Component only patches imagePullPolicy). There is no mechanism producing a third counted container.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: A user watching `kubectl get pods` sees 2/2, concludes the deploy is broken, and debugs a non-problem. No functional impact (the deploy is healthy; smoke test and Ready watch still pass), so it is low.
confirmed_findings.fix: Change 3/3 to 2/2 in the verify table and add a note: "head pod = ray-head + autoscaler sidecar (injected by KubeRay because enableInTreeAutoscaling is enabled)." Optionally assert the head container count in CI.

confirmed_findings.title: KubeRay operator installed unpinned in CI while README pins 1.3.0
confirmed_findings.problem: CI runs `helm install kuberay-operator ...` with no --version (always latest chart) while k8s/README.md:70 instructs --version 1.3.0. CI validates a different operator than the docs prescribe; a future mutable chart release can flip a green e2e to red with no repo change. NOTE: the original finding's bundled kustomize sub-claim was substantially REFUTED — kubectl's embedded Kustomize v5.8.1 satisfies the README's 5.x+ prereq and `kubectl apply -k` renders identical manifests to standalone `kustomize build`, so there is no toolchain divergence. Also the "docs' 1.3.0 path can break while CI stays green" direction is logically void (a frozen chart cannot spontaneously break).
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Nondeterministic CI against a mutable upstream dependency, and CI does not exercise the version the docs recommend. Low-cost reproducibility fix; no deploy/security impact.
confirmed_findings.fix: Pin `--version 1.3.0` on the helm install in k8s-integration-test.yml (or source both it and the README from one shared variable). Drop the kustomize half of the finding. Optionally standardize the README Quick Start on one kustomize invocation for tidiness (non-blocking).

confirmed_findings.title: Resources gym-server logs to /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log (invisible to kubectl logs); CI "Resources Logs" step shows raylet output instead
confirmed_findings.problem: The resources server is a postStart child whose stdout/stderr is redirected to /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log; since PID 1 is the raylet, `kubectl logs` shows only raylet output. The CI failure-diagnostics step's `=== Resources Logs ===` uses `kubectl logs -l ...gym-resources` and therefore shows raylet output, not the gym server — a mislabeled/misleading diagnostic. NOTE: harm is bounded — the README documents the correct retrieval (`kubectl exec ... cat /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log`), probes + the wait-for-Ready CI gate make a broken server fail loudly, and the log path is exec-readable (not truly invisible); the spill-contention sub-claim is weak (gym-resources is num-cpus=0).
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: The single most useful debugging log for a failed resources server is not surfaced where operators and CI look. Cheap to fix; low severity.
confirmed_findings.fix: Fix the CI diagnostics step to `kubectl exec` and cat /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log (or redirect the server to the pod's stdout, e.g. tee /dev/stdout or /proc/1/fd/1). If sending to stdout, also rotate/cap it so it cannot consume the ray-tmp emptyDir budget.

confirmed_findings.title: No NetworkPolicy; README "complete isolation / no data bleed" overclaims for untrusted-code workloads
confirmed_findings.problem: There is no NetworkPolicy anywhere under k8s/, and the integration test's kindnet CNI does not enforce one. Isolation is achieved only via namePrefix + label selectors. A compromised/malicious benchmark pod (e.g. code_gen's gym-resources/gym-workers running untrusted candidate code) can reach another instance's Ray GCS/dashboard/client and the credentialed model/head/agent services. k8s/README.md:171 says namePrefix+labels ensure "complete isolation — no resource name collisions, no service cross-selection, no data bleed," which a reader concerned with untrusted code will read more broadly than the naming-scoped intent. NOTE: the original files list padded root README.md — the claim lives ONLY at k8s/README.md:171.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Real (if marginal-over-F1) lateral-movement surface given the threat model includes running arbitrary code, plus a genuine docs overclaim. A base NetworkPolicy would be inert under kindnet and an egress allowlist to POLICY_BASE_URL cannot be hardcoded (per-deployment), so segmentation is legitimately an operator concern — keeping this low and primarily a documentation fix.
confirmed_findings.fix: Soften k8s/README.md:171 to scope the guarantee to resource-naming isolation and explicitly note it does NOT provide network-level isolation without a policy-enforcing CNI and per-instance policies. Optionally ship an opt-in default-deny + intra-instance-allow NetworkPolicy (keyed on app.kubernetes.io/instance) in a separate overlay/component, documenting the CNI requirement and operator-supplied egress. Remove the padded root README.md citation; consider merging the network aspect into F1 to avoid double-counting.

confirmed_findings.title: Troubleshooting curl expands $POLICY_BASE_URL/$POLICY_API_KEY on the local client, not in the pod
confirmed_findings.problem: `kubectl exec deploy/cg-gym-model -n gym -- curl -s "$POLICY_BASE_URL/models" -H "Authorization: Bearer $POLICY_API_KEY"` has no in-pod shell wrapper, so the user's LOCAL shell expands the variables before kubectl runs. Those values live only in the in-cluster gym-secrets Secret (via envFrom; the explicit model env block does not redeclare them), so in a fresh shell both expand empty and the command degrades to `curl -s "/models" -H "Authorization: Bearer "` — a bare/schemeless path curl rejects.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: The documented diagnostic for testing in-cluster LLM reachability always fails and tests nothing — the opposite of its purpose. Fails loudly (visible curl error), so low.
confirmed_findings.fix: Wrap in a remote shell so the variables resolve in-pod: `kubectl exec deploy/cg-gym-model -n gym -- sh -c 'curl -s "$POLICY_BASE_URL/models" -H "Authorization: Bearer $POLICY_API_KEY"'`.

confirmed_findings.title: "Pre-built images are available from GHCR" is premature — :latest does not exist until merge to main
confirmed_findings.problem: README Quick Start step 1 states in present tense that pre-built GHCR images are available, but the build workflow pushes only on non-PR events and tags :latest only on the default branch. So :latest does not exist until this PR merges to main; a PR-branch reviewer or fork user following the docs literally hits ImagePullBackOff. The image paths themselves are correct and match the manifests. (Overlaps F9; tracked separately because F28 is purely the docs caveat.)
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: First-time users on a fork or pre-merge get a stuck deploy with a non-obvious cause and no docs mention of the timing or of GHCR package visibility defaults.
confirmed_findings.fix: Change README:77 to a conditional caveat ("GHCR images are published after this lands on main; on a fork or before merge, build from source — below"). Also note that newly-created GHCR packages default to private, so even post-merge an unauthenticated pull fails until visibility is set public (relevant since the OpenShift overlay sets imagePullPolicy: Always). No manifest change needed.

confirmed_findings.title: Ray reinstalled unpinned (and redundantly) in the resources/all-in-one image stages
confirmed_findings.problem: raycluster.yaml hardcodes rayVersion 2.55.1, but the resources and all-in-one stages run `pip install --no-cache-dir "ray[default]" numpy==2.2.6` after base-runtime already installed ray via `pip install -e .`. NOTE: the original "risks pulling a newer Ray than rayVersion / breaks autoscaler/fault-tolerance" framing is overstated — with no -U and no specifier the ray reinstall is a no-op (only numpy==2.2.6 is operative), all Ray components run from one image so no inter-component skew is possible, and KubeRay uses rayVersion mainly for autoscaler v1/v2 selection, not hard runtime enforcement.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Build-hygiene/maintainability: a wasteful reinstall line plus a real-but-different drift surface (a :latest rebuild can resolve ray newer than the hardcoded rayVersion via pyproject's >=2.55.1 floor — which the redundant line does not fix).
confirmed_findings.fix: Drop the redundant `ray[default]` token from Dockerfile:50,74 (keep numpy==2.2.6 only if the transitive pin is actually needed, and document why). To genuinely prevent image-vs-rayVersion skew on :latest rebuilds, pin ray to exactly 2.55.1 via a single build ARG matching raycluster.yaml:10 (or move off :latest to a digest/version tag). Drop the unsupported autoscaler/fault-tolerance framing.

confirmed_findings.title: Probes target FastAPI /docs (static Swagger page) rather than a semantic health endpoint
confirmed_findings.problem: Every startup/readiness/liveness probe targets path:/docs. All servers use plain FastAPI() with default docs enabled, so /docs returns 200 as soon as uvicorn accepts connections — a superficial health signal that can keep a wedged-but-listening server alive and route traffic to one that cannot serve /run. For the head image /docs is the only stable GET path. NOTE: the original 'high' severity and its recommendation rest on wrong-tree evidence — the cited `GET /` liveness endpoint (server_utils.py:380) did NOT exist on the PR branch (introduced later by PR #1431; merge-base proves it postdates the PR's last main merge), and `GET /` returns a constant {"status":"ok"} just as oblivious to Ray/backend health as /docs.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Unidiomatic, low-quality health signal; the probes function (do not crash-loop), so this is a quality issue, not a broken deploy. The author's /docs choice was defensible given what existed on their branch.
confirmed_findings.fix: Now that PR #1431 has merged `GET /` into SimpleServer/HeadServer on main, repoint readiness/liveness at `GET /` for agent/model/resources and head; reserve /docs for the startupProbe if used at all. Be explicit this does not deepen the check (both return a constant 200) — a future, deeper health route should verify Ray/backend reachability, but that is out of scope here.

confirmed_findings.title: postStart raylet-socket wait is an unbounded loop with an opaque slow-failure mode
confirmed_findings.problem: The postStart hook runs `( until [ -S /tmp/ray/session_latest/sockets/raylet ]; do sleep 1; done; ...; exec $NEMO_GYM_SERVER_ENTRYPOINT >> /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log 2>&1 ) &`. The until-loop has no timeout and is backgrounded, so postStart returns immediately and the container reports Running even if the socket never appears. Mitigated by the startupProbe (~330s), so not a deploy-breaker. NOTE: two supporting claims were corrected — the log is exec-readable (not kubectl-invisible), and the common `ray start` failure crashes the KubeRay raylet (visible in kubectl logs); the 'Running but socket never appears' path is the rare raylet-hang case.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Minor robustness/diagnostics: a failure in the rare hang case surfaces slowly via the ~330s startup window. Do NOT apply the originally proposed bounded `for…break` loop verbatim — without a post-loop guard it would fall through to exec and launch the server against a missing raylet (strictly worse).
confirmed_findings.fix: If improving: on a bounded timeout, write a clear error line to the log and exit non-zero (`[ -S ... ] || { echo 'raylet socket never appeared'; exit 1; }`) so failure surfaces before the startup-probe window. Optionally tee the server log to stdout so it appears in kubectl logs.

confirmed_findings.title: Overlays patch RayCluster env by absolute array index (brittle positional coupling); base placeholder does not fail fast
confirmed_findings.problem: Two overlays mutate the resources-group entrypoint/config via JSON6902 op:replace addressed by absolute index (.../env/2/value, .../env/4/value). Reordering env[0]-env[4] in base or adding a worker group silently mis-patches with no error. The base ships non-fail-fast placeholders (`python -m http.server 9080`, `MUST_BE_SET_BY_OVERLAY`). NOTE: the original scope/remedy were overstated — the env-index patches exist in exactly TWO overlays (NOT the openshift Component, which uses index patches only for imagePullPolicy; duplicate_count is 2, not 3), and a name-keyed strategic-merge fix is INFEASIBLE against the RayCluster CRD (no patchMergeKey schema), and even the existing replacements style still hard-codes the worker-group index '.1'.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Maintainability/fragility; current renders are correct and a mis-patch fails loudly (the /docs startupProbe 404s against the placeholder http.server), so it is latent. The author's explicit WARNING comment is good.
confirmed_findings.fix: Drop the infeasible strategic-merge recommendation. Adopt the sound remedies: add a CI assertion that rendered manifests contain the expected env[2] entrypoint and env[4]=resources_instance (extend the reward==1 e2e to code-gen and/or add a kubectl kustomize grep), and make the base placeholder fail fast (exit non-zero with a clear "NEMO_GYM_SERVER_ENTRYPOINT must be set by overlay" message) instead of a /docs-404-only http.server. Keep the WARNING comment.

confirmed_findings.title: Base (unprefixed) Service selectors are name-only — latent cross-selection if base is applied alongside an overlay in one namespace
confirmed_findings.problem: Base Service selectors are name-only (e.g. {app.kubernetes.io/name: gym-resources}); the per-instance app.kubernetes.io/instance label is injected only by the overlay. Applying base directly is internally consistent for a single instance and two base applies in one namespace collide on name. But base + an overlay co-resident in the SAME namespace is reachable with no name collision, and the base's name-only gym-resources-svc would then erroneously select the overlay's pods (which retain name=gym-resources) — a genuine cross-instance selection that technically violates the README's "no service cross-selection" guarantee.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Latent footgun on an undocumented misuse path; impact limited to traffic mis-routing during ad-hoc experimentation (no security/data-loss). Base is documented as overlay-only, so it is not active.
confirmed_findings.fix: Add a one-line note in base/kustomization.yaml and/or k8s/README.md that k8s/base must only be consumed via an overlay and never applied into a namespace that also hosts overlay instances. Optionally add a sentinel app.kubernetes.io/instance to the base labels+selectors so a direct apply is self-consistent. Scope README:171's "no service cross-selection" claim to the overlay-only workflow.

confirmed_findings.title: Editable install + readOnlyRootFilesystem causes silent first-party __pycache__ write failures; PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE unset
confirmed_findings.problem: Images use `pip install -e .` so nemo_gym source lives on the read-only root; at runtime readOnlyRootFilesystem:true with only /tmp writable means CPython cannot write __pycache__ next to the first-party source. CPython degrades gracefully (no crash), so cold starts recompile first-party bytecode without caching, and the read-only-root behavior is an accident of CPython's fallback rather than declared intent. NOTE: pip precompiles the dependency wheels into the image, so only the small first-party nemo_gym tree recompiles — the original 'no caching across the board' framing is overstated.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Performance/hygiene: a tiny one-time first-party recompile per cold start, amortized over a long-lived pod. Cheap, correct fix.
confirmed_findings.fix: Set `ENV PYTHONPYCACHEPREFIX=/tmp/pycache` (so caching succeeds in the only writable dir) or `ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1` in the Dockerfile, OR build and install a wheel (with `python -m compileall`) so bytecode is baked at build time. Reframe to first-party-only recompile.

confirmed_findings.title: Resources pod's /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log shares the 4Gi ray-tmp emptyDir with the Ray session dir and grows unbounded
confirmed_findings.file: k8s/base/raycluster.yaml (also deployment-head/agent/model tmp sizing, which is fine)
confirmed_findings.problem: On the gym-resources pod, the postStart hook appends to /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log with no rotation, RAY_TMPDIR=/tmp points Ray's session dir at the same /tmp, and /tmp is the ray-tmp emptyDir with sizeLimit:4Gi. An append-only server log can accrete over a long-lived pod and, with the Ray session dir, approach the 4Gi cap (best-effort eviction could then kill the pod). NOTE: scope correction — ONLY gym-resources holds this log (head and gym-workers have no postStart hook), and the log-bearing pod runs num-cpus=0 so the 'spill + log collide' premise is weak; the 100Mi head and 1Gi agent/model sizes are fine.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Slow-burn resource exhaustion on the one pod that writes the log; bounded and low-likelihood.
confirmed_findings.fix: Redirect the postStart entrypoint output to container stdout/stderr (drop `>> /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log 2>&1`, letting kubectl logs capture it) or rotate/cap the file so it cannot accrete into the gym-resources 4Gi budget. Do not frame as a spill+log collision filling 4Gi.

confirmed_findings.title: /etc/passwd arbitrary-UID fallback is a no-op under readOnlyRootFilesystem; chmod g=u /etc/passwd is inert at runtime
confirmed_findings.problem: entrypoint.sh appends an nss line to /etc/passwd only `if [ -w /etc/passwd ]`, but every runtime pod sets readOnlyRootFilesystem:true with only /tmp writable, so /etc/passwd is read-only and the append never runs; the Dockerfile's `chmod g=u /etc/passwd` is therefore inert in the deployed config. NOTE: the original 'security/medium' framing is wrong — no manifest injects a random runAsUser (only runAsNonRoot:true; image bakes USER 1001 which exists in UBI9's /etc/passwd, so whoami succeeds and the fallback is dormant, not broken), the load-bearing arbitrary-UID hardening (chgrp/chmod on /opt/app-root + ENV HOME) works, and the 'getpwuid KeyError' claim cites no actual caller.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Maintainability/docs: the passwd-append branch never fires in any shipped config and implies functionality that does not run. Not a security vulnerability and not a crash.
confirmed_findings.fix: Add a comment that the /etc/passwd append is intentionally a no-op under readOnlyRootFilesystem and that the real arbitrary-UID hardening is the chgrp/chmod on /opt/app-root plus ENV HOME. If true OpenShift random-UID support without a writable root is a goal, use nss_wrapper or a writable projected/emptyDir passwd. Keep `chmod g=u /etc/passwd` (idiomatic, harmless). Do not block the PR on this.

confirmed_findings.title: all-in-one Dockerfile target has no CMD and is never built or referenced — dead/incomplete code
confirmed_findings.problem: The all-in-one stage installs ray+numpy, re-applies perms, and ends with only `EXPOSE 8080` — no CMD. It is never built (the build matrix and README cover only resources/agent/model/head) and no manifest or test references it. Despite the `# --- All-in-one (testing) ---` label, k8s/tests/ uses a separate mock_model image, so the comment is not wired to any test.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Undocumented, unbuilt, untested dead code. NOTE: precise symptom — with the inherited entrypoint.sh `exec "$@"` and no CMD, an all-in-one container would exec an empty command and silently exit 0 (no-op), not crash.
confirmed_findings.fix: Either delete the all-in-one stage, or give it a working CMD AND wire it into a build/test (e.g. point k8s/tests/ at it and add it to the build matrix). Correct the 'would fail if run' wording to 'silently exits 0 / runs no server.'

confirmed_findings.title: E2E smoke-test reward assertion is non-discriminating (example_single_tool_call verify() returns reward=1.0 unconditionally) and the tool path is never exercised
confirmed_findings.problem: The smoke test asserts reward==1.0, but example_single_tool_call's verifier returns reward=1.0 unconditionally (pre-existing stub) and the mock model emits no function_call, so get_weather is never invoked. NOTE: the original 'tautological / gives false confidence about the deployment' framing is overstated — the assertion is NOT satisfied unconditionally (curl -sf + if/else exit 1 fails on an unreachable round-trip), the gym-resources readiness probe is discriminating, and simple_agent always runs seed_session→responses→verify, so the cross-pod round-trip IS exercised. No broken deployment yields a false PASS.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: The assertion is mislabeled (reward read as a correctness signal it cannot be) and the tool-calling path/the resources /get_weather endpoint are never covered — a narrow coverage/clarity gap, not a deployment false-positive.
confirmed_findings.fix: Add a comment at the reward assertion noting the verifier returns a constant 1.0 by design so the test proves cross-service reachability, not scoring. Optionally have the mock emit a get_weather function_call to cover the tool path.

confirmed_findings.title: k8s/head/entrypoint.py lacks the SPDX/Apache license header
confirmed_findings.problem: Many new k8s files lack SPDX headers, but the repo's copyright-check CI (_copyright_check.yml@v0.55.0, invoked with no with: block) matches only .py files and excludes __init__. Of the two .py files in the subtree, k8s/tests/mock_model.py already has the full header and k8s/head/entrypoint.py does not. The repo itself ships YAML without SPDX (e.g. example_single_tool_call.yaml), so the YAML/Dockerfile/sh/md files are out of scope. NOTE: the original '21 of 22 files' count is inaccurate (~30 unheadered total; only one is CI-enforced).
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: CI copyright-check will flag exactly k8s/head/entrypoint.py and block merge.
confirmed_findings.fix: Add the NVIDIA Apache-2.0 SPDX header to k8s/head/entrypoint.py. Do not require SPDX on the YAML manifests, Dockerfile, Containerfile, sh, or md files (CI does not check them and the repo ships YAML without headers). Optional comment headers on Dockerfile/entrypoint.sh are a nit.

confirmed_findings.title: Base-OS divergence (UBI9 main vs UBI10-minimal head) has no documented justification
confirmed_findings.problem: The four main images use ubi9/python-312; the head uses ubi10/python-312-minimal, with no stated reason (k8s/README.md:34 only says it is 'separate', not why), adding CVE-tracking surface across two RHEL majors. NOTE: the original 'largely negating minimal-base savings' and 'lazy-import ray to slim the head' claims were refuted — the minimal base saves at the OS layer (orthogonal to the identical Python dep tree), and `pip install -e .` installs ray[default] from pyproject regardless of import placement, so lazy import would not slim the image. The 'lightweight' label is accurate as a payload description (head copies only nemo_gym/, omitting the three server dirs).
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Low-value maintainability/docs nit.
confirmed_findings.fix: Standardize on one UBI major OR add a one-line comment in head/Containerfile explaining the choice. Drop the 'negating savings' and 'lazy-import ray' framing.

confirmed_findings.title: Resources K8s config is never validated under NEMO_GYM_CONFIG_DICT (dangling server-refs fail only at request time)
confirmed_findings.problem: When a server boots from NEMO_GYM_CONFIG_DICT, get_global_config_dict() returns OmegaConf.create(env) with no server-ref validation (vs ng_run's parse(), which raises ServerRefNotFoundError and defaults host/port). The base configmap's agent references resources_instance with no such server in base. NOTE: the original 'removes a safety net' framing is inaccurate — the env-var path is documented to skip validation by design (predates the PR), base is incomplete in several other ways too (POLICY_* secret, worker entrypoint env[2]/[4]), and the same partial config would also fail under ng_run, just earlier. Only the timing differs (request-time KeyError vs parse-time error).
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Minor robustness/DX: on the supported overlay path, a typo'd NEMO_GYM_CONFIG_PATH or renamed server yields an opaque request-time KeyError instead of a clear parse error.
confirmed_findings.fix: Add a one-line comment in base/configmap.yaml that the base is an incomplete template and overlays MUST supply resources_instance + POLICY_* secret + worker entrypoint. Optionally add a lightweight startup self-check that resolves the agent's resources/model refs and exits with a clear error. Drop the 'removed safety net' wording.

confirmed_findings.title: Per-target gha cache scopes prevent sharing the expensive base-build/base-runtime layers across resources/agent/model builds
confirmed_findings.problem: resources/agent/model share heavy base-build/base-runtime stages but each matrix leg uses an isolated `scope=${{ matrix.target }}` gha cache, so on a cold cache the shared base layers are rebuilt per target. NOTE: this is the canonical Docker matrix pattern (a single shared scope written by parallel legs would race/clobber under gha last-write-wins), the legs run in parallel so the cost is CPU-minutes not wall-clock, and the proposed shared-scope fix would force a serial prior job.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Optional CI-cost cleanup on cold/changed-input runs only; the current choice is defensible.
confirmed_findings.fix: Leave as-is for this PR. If maintainers later want to trim cost, add a prior needs:-gated job that builds base-runtime into a shared scope=base once and has per-target legs cache-from it. Soften the 'rebuilt 3x' wording to 'redundant base builds across parallel legs (CPU-minutes, cold-cache only).'

confirmed_findings.title: Two pip install -e . invocations across base-build and base-runtime (not actually redundant)
confirmed_findings.problem: base-build runs `pip install -e .` with only nemo_gym/ present; base-runtime copies the populated lib and re-runs `pip install -e .`. NOTE: the original 'redundant / same value / re-resolves the dependency graph / contacts the index' framing is refuted — base-runtime additionally copies resources_servers/, responses_api_agents/, responses_api_models/ first, so its editable install registers a materially broader package set (per pyproject [tool.setuptools.packages.find]) required by the server CMDs' cross-package imports; with deps already present pip skips network for satisfied pins.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Clarity/maintainability nit only; the two installs are not redundant.
confirmed_findings.fix: Optionally add `--no-deps --no-index` (and `--no-build-isolation`) to the base-runtime install to make it an explicit metadata/registration-only step. Rewrite the rationale: the second install registers the broader package set, it is not duplicate work.

confirmed_findings.title: Published images are amd64-only (no platforms: key); ARM nodes cannot pull
confirmed_findings.problem: The build step (docker/build-push-action@v6, lines 80-91) has no `platforms:` key, so on ubuntu-latest buildx publishes amd64-only manifests; ARM nodes (e.g. Graviton) fail to pull with no-matching-manifest. NOTE: the original evidence string ('only amd64 token in diff') misattributed the cause to a Containerfile build-recipe comment; the real cause is the absent platforms: key. UBI bases are already multi-arch.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Real low-severity portability gap for a PR targeting cloud k8s where ARM is mainstream. Nothing regresses (greenfield), and it is reasonable to defer given CI emulation cost.
confirmed_findings.fix: Add `platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64` to the build step AND a `docker/setup-qemu-action@v3` step (currently missing in both workflows; setup-buildx alone does not provide QEMU). Acceptable as an incremental follow-up.

confirmed_findings.title: Third-party actions pinned to floating major tags rather than commit SHAs (privileged push workflow)
confirmed_findings.problem: All third-party actions use floating major tags (docker/* @v3/v5/v6, helm/kind-action@v1); build-k8s-images.yml holds packages:write and pushes to GHCR on main/tags. NOTE: the original 'established convention these workflows do not follow' justification is INVERTED by the repo data — the repo is ~90% floating-tag (only 2 first-party actions/* are SHA-pinned, no documented policy), so these workflows match the dominant convention.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Defense-in-depth, not a convention divergence: a compromised upstream tag could affect the privileged push job on main/tags (fork-PR exposure is limited since login/push are gated to non-pull_request).
confirmed_findings.fix: Optional hardening: SHA-pin at least docker/login-action and docker/build-push-action (with trailing # vX.Y.Z, Dependabot/Renovate-updatable). Correct the justification — do not claim SHA-pinning is an established repo convention.

confirmed_findings.title: No image signing / SBOM / provenance in the build workflow
confirmed_findings.problem: The build-and-push step has no provenance:/sbom: keys and no cosign step; build-push-action@v6 disables provenance by default on GitHub Actions runners, so pushed images likely carry no attestation. NOTE: this matches the repo's existing _build_container.yml (which also lacks these), so it is parity-with-repo, not a regression.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Optional supply-chain hardening; low blast radius (repo-scoped GHCR, non-PR events only).
confirmed_findings.fix: If adopted: set `provenance: mode=max` and `sbom: true` on the build step, and optionally add keyless cosign signing (id-token: write). Non-blocking; note parity with the existing image pipeline.

confirmed_findings.title: No imagePullSecrets / private-registry guidance; ImagePullBackOff if GHCR packages are private
confirmed_findings.problem: gym-sa has no imagePullSecrets and no pod-level pull secrets exist; the README has no private-registry/auth guidance. GHCR package visibility cannot be proven from the repo and newly-pushed packages commonly default to private — if private, every Deployment + RayCluster worker would ImagePullBackOff on any non-CI cluster with a non-obvious cause. CI (kind load + IfNotPresent) never exercises a registry pull, so green CI does not validate auth. (Conditional on external package settings; overlaps F9/F28.)
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Plausible stuck-deploy for first-time users on a real cluster, with no docs pointer. Conditional and not a base-manifest defect.
confirmed_findings.fix: Do NOT add a guessed imagePullSecrets name to base/serviceaccount.yaml (no Secret to reference). Add a 'Private registry' note to k8s/README.md showing how to create a dockerconfigjson Secret and patch it onto gym-sa via an overlay. Ensure the built GHCR packages are actually published public (the openshift overlay's imagePullPolicy: Always amplifies this for private registries).

confirmed_findings.title: No Prometheus scrape path (no ServiceMonitor / scrape annotations); server logs only to an emptyDir
confirmed_findings.problem: No ServiceMonitor and no prometheus.io/scrape annotations exist anywhere in k8s/, so Ray's metrics on 8080 and the FastAPI servers are not scraped out of the box; server logs go only to /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log on an emptyDir (unstructured, lost on restart). NOTE: the original 'port 8265 unexposed' evidence is FALSE — the Ray dashboard IS exposed (dashboard-host 0.0.0.0, containerPort 8265) and documented via port-forward; omitting a base ServiceMonitor is also a defensible default (a monitoring.coreos.com/v1 object would break apply on clusters without the Prometheus Operator CRD).
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Genuine but minor out-of-box observability gap; the interactive Ray dashboard provides a documented operability story.
confirmed_findings.fix: Optional, non-blocking: ship a ServiceMonitor as a separate overlay/Component (not base, to avoid breaking apply without the Prometheus Operator CRD) plus scrape annotations, and optionally log to stdout for aggregation. Correct the evidence — 8265 is exposed and documented.

confirmed_findings.title: Deployment targets external LLM only (no in-cluster GPU/vLLM, training token-id capture disabled); not documented as a scope limitation
confirmed_findings.problem: There is no in-cluster inference path: the model pod is a CPU-only OpenAI-compatible proxy to POLICY_BASE_URL, NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES: void on all Ray containers, and configmap.yaml:42 sets return_token_id_information: false. So the subtree is eval-against-hosted-API only and does not cover NeMo Gym's flagship local-vLLM/training (token-id) workflow, but the README never flags this as a limitation. NOTE: the original 'gpu' framing and 'vLLM cannot run' claim are inaccurate — `void` is the correct sentinel for CPU-only pods and vllm_model is by design a client proxy that runs fine.
confirmed_findings.why_it_matters: Sets correct expectations for a first optional k8s contribution; users centered on training/open-weight serving need to know this path is unsupported.
confirmed_findings.fix: Add a short 'Scope / Limitations' note to k8s/README.md stating this deployment targets evaluation against an external OpenAI-compatible endpoint only (no in-cluster GPU/open-weight serving, training token-id capture disabled). Optionally note a GPU in-cluster inference overlay as future work. Drop the 'gpu' framing and 'vLLM cannot run' claim.

dismissed_findings.title: F24 — Head entrypoint.sh #!/bin/bash may not run on python-312-minimal
dismissed_findings.why_dismissed: REFUTED empirically. Pulling the exact base image registry.access.redhat.com/ubi10/python-312-minimal shows /bin/bash and /usr/bin/bash are both present (1.4MB), and CI builds+rolls out the head pod (its #!/bin/bash entrypoint must exec for the test to pass). Moreover the proposed #!/bin/sh fix is unsafe: entrypoint.sh:2 uses `whoami &> /dev/null`, and bash vs dash diverge on that line, which would flip the arbitrary-UID branch. The author's #!/bin/bash is correct. Optional only: `microdnf install -y bash` to make the dependency explicit.

dismissed_findings.title: C7 — No PodDisruptionBudget / PriorityClass / HPA availability primitives
dismissed_findings.why_dismissed: REFUTED on substance. 'No HPA' is false and the recommendation is harmful — KubeRay in-tree autoscaling is already enabled (enableInTreeAutoscaling:true, gym-workers minReplicas:1/maxReplicas:4); a CPU-target HPA would fight the Ray autoscaler. QoS is intentionally Burstable (memory limits set, CPU limits omitted), a best practice, and orthogonal to PDBs. A PDB on the single-replica Deployments/worker groups cannot protect work (minAvailable:1 blocks drains; maxUnavailable:1 is a no-op). Graceful-shutdown levers (preStop sleep, terminationGracePeriodSeconds, maxUnavailable:0) are already present. Only a nit-level residual (the single gym-resources worker loses in-flight work on a voluntary drain) remains, mitigated by re-runnability; the proposed fixes don't fit single-replica batch components.

dismissed_findings.title: C2 — No PVC; rollout outputs on emptyDir lost on restart
dismissed_findings.why_dismissed: Persistence/data-loss premise refuted. The deployed pods are stateless HTTP services that never write rollout artifacts to disk — rollouts are produced by ng_collect_rollouts on the operator's machine via port-forward to a client-side path. The emptyDirs hold only ephemeral Ray scratch and the relaunchable server log. readOnlyRootFilesystem + writable /tmp is intentional hardening, and a PVC would conflict with the autoscaling worker group (RWO single-attach). Only a minor observability note survives (the /tmp server log not appearing in kubectl logs and lost on restart), already partly covered by F5 and the README; 'Add PVC' is rejected.

dismissed_findings.title: F32 — No CPU limits (Burstable QoS) and RayCluster does not honor Deployment rollingUpdate semantics
dismissed_findings.why_dismissed: Net non-issue. The CPU-request-without-limit Burstable QoS is a deliberate, defensible choice (avoids CFS throttling), and the manifests ship no LimitRange/Namespace so the LimitRange-injection caveat is hypothetical. The cross-API rolling-update difference is not a defect — the RayCluster CRD has no rollingUpdate field, so the manifests make no false zero-downtime promise. The only durable nugget is an optional one-line doc note that RayCluster pods are reconciled by KubeRay rather than Deployment maxUnavailable:0 semantics. No action required for merge.

dismissed_findings.title: F29 — Architecture diagram / data-flow text is code_gen-specific
dismissed_findings.why_dismissed: Confirmed but lowest-severity nit, folded into the docs-accuracy cluster rather than tracked separately. k8s/README.md:26's 'agent extracts code → Ray workers execute unit tests' is code_gen-specific and inaccurate for example_single_tool_call, but the harm is mild (the architecture box already shows both cg- and estc- instances and the Verify table documents the worker pods). Worth a small wording fix (generic '/verify scores the task' plus a code_gen-specific note); not a blocker.

dismissed_findings.title: F30 — Two similarly-named head services (gym-head-svc vs KubeRay's <cluster>-head-svc)
dismissed_findings.why_dismissed: Confirmed-but-rejected-remedy nit. There is no name collision; the names differ by the meaningful -ray- infix, are on distinct ports (11000 vs 8265), and live in distinct README sections, so confusion is unlikely. The proposed rename has real blast radius (head Service selector label, deployment-head labels, overlay replacements) for zero functional gain and should NOT be done. The only worthwhile action is the optional one-line doc note that cg-gym-ray-head-svc is auto-created by KubeRay — non-blocking.

open_questions: GHCR package visibility for ghcr.io/nvidia-nemo/gym/nemo-gym-{resources,agent,model,head}: are these packages set public? This is governed outside the repo and cannot be confirmed from the diff. If private, F9/F28/C6 escalate from docs caveats to a real ImagePullBackOff on any non-CI cluster (CI uses kind load + IfNotPresent and never validates the pull/auth path).

Is the OpenShift overlay actually exercised anywhere? CI runs only on kind (kindnet, no SCC, runs as UID 1001), so the OpenShift-specific paths (arbitrary random-UID handling per F21, imagePullPolicy: Always) are never validated end-to-end. Has the author deployed the openshift overlay on a real OpenShift cluster?

Is encrypted intra-cluster transport an actual goal (which would make F1 a missing feature to implement) or was 'mTLS-authenticated' purely loose wording to delete? The docs give no roadmap signal either way.

ng_reward_profile's behavior against the standalone in-cluster HeadServer was not verified (F18). The README lists it among cluster-working CLI tools, but only ng_collect_rollouts (via /global_config_dict_yaml) and ng_status (broken) were traced. Does ng_reward_profile rely on /server_instances (and thus also report nothing)?
strengths: Comprehensive, consistent pod-level security hardening on every container: runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, allowPrivilegeEscalation:false, capabilities drop ALL, seccompProfile RuntimeDefault — a genuinely above-average baseline for a community k8s contribution.

Real multi-instance isolation via per-overlay namePrefix + app.kubernetes.io/instance labels with includeSelectors, plus kustomize replacements and CRD name-reference propagation, correctly preventing resource-name collisions and Service cross-selection so multiple benchmarks can co-exist in one namespace (network-level segmentation is the documented caveat, F16).

An actual end-to-end kind integration test (helm-install KubeRay → build+load 4 images → roll out agent/model/head + RayCluster → port-forward → assert a full cross-pod round-trip) that exercises the genuinely novel mechanism of this PR: running the resources server inside a Ray worker group via a postStart raylet-socket-wait launch.

Thoughtful graceful-shutdown and rollout configuration on the Deployments (maxUnavailable:0/maxSurge:1, minReadySeconds, preStop sleep, terminationGracePeriodSeconds) and correct use of KubeRay in-tree autoscaling for the compute worker group.

Deliberate arbitrary-UID / OpenShift support (chgrp/chmod g=u on /opt/app-root, ENV HOME, an openshift overlay Component) and a sensible client-proxy model that keeps GPU/inference out of cluster, reusing the existing initialize_ray() client-connect path.

Clean greenfield scoping: the entire feature lives in an optional k8s/ subtree and two new workflows, touches the core library minimally (the NEMO_GYM_SKIP_RAY_INIT short-circuit), gitignores secrets correctly with a committed .example template, and is well-documented overall — the issues found are predominantly documentation-accuracy fixes rather than design flaws.

The author demonstrably understood the constraints: explicit WARNING comments on the brittle env-index patches, a documented kubectl-exec path for the file-based server log, a non-fail-fast guard that no-ops rather than errors on read-only root, and a port choice (9080) made specifically to avoid Ray's metrics port — these show care even where the chosen tradeoffs are debatable.

concept_map.slurm_analogy: A single allocated task slot that wraps one or more co-scheduled processes — closest to one `srun` step running on one node, but the unit of scheduling itself.
concept_map.explanation: A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in K8s: one or more containers that always run together on the SAME node, sharing a network namespace (same localhost, same Pod IP) and shared volumes. In SLURM, the scheduler hands you a node allocation and you launch processes onto it; in K8s, the Pod IS the allocation-plus-process bundle, and the scheduler (kube-scheduler) decides which node it lands on. Where the analogy breaks: a SLURM step is ephemeral and you control its launch directly; a Pod is a declared object the cluster keeps trying to place and (often) keeps restarting. In THIS PR the `gym-resources` worker Pod is the most unusual: PID 1 is `ray start --block` (the raylet), and the actual NeMo Gym resources FastAPI server is a *second* process launched by a `postStart` hook into the background — so one Pod runs two logically separate services. That co-location is exactly why finding F4 bites: a liveness probe failure on the FastAPI child restarts the whole Pod (raylet included).

concept_map.slurm_analogy: A self-resubmitting batch job with `--requeue` on steroids: you declare 'I want N copies of this service running forever,' and a controller keeps resubmitting/replacing them to maintain that count, including rolling restarts on config change.
concept_map.explanation: A Deployment is a controller object that manages a long-running, stateless service. You give it a Pod template and a replica count; its job is to make reality match that declaration continuously. Unlike `sbatch` (fire-and-forget: you submit, it runs to completion, it's done), a Deployment never 'completes' — if a Pod dies, it's recreated; if you change the Pod template (new image, new env), it performs a rolling update: spin up new Pods, wait for them Ready, then tear down old ones. THIS PR uses Deployments for the three stateless HTTP servers: `gym-agent`, `gym-model`, `gym-head`. The Ray nodes are NOT Deployments — they're managed by the RayCluster CRD instead. Where it breaks: SLURM jobs are imperative and time-bounded; Deployments are declarative and unbounded. The RollingUpdate strategy here (`maxSurge:1, maxUnavailable:0`) means zero-downtime swaps — there's no clean SLURM analog because SLURM has no notion of 'keep the old one serving until the new one is healthy.'

concept_map.slurm_analogy: The `--array=0-N` (job array) count, but enforced continuously rather than submitted once. `replicas: 3` is like guaranteeing exactly 3 array tasks are alive at all times.
concept_map.explanation: A Deployment doesn't manage Pods directly — it creates a ReplicaSet, and the ReplicaSet's only job is 'keep exactly N matching Pods alive.' Each rolling update creates a NEW ReplicaSet (new Pod template) and scales the old one to zero. You rarely touch ReplicaSets directly; you reason in terms of the Deployment's `replicas` field. In THIS PR every Deployment sets `replicas: 1` (single agent, single model, single head) — it's a reference deployment, not a scaled-out production one. The Ray worker groups use a different but analogous concept (`replicas`/`minReplicas`/`maxReplicas` in the RayCluster spec). Where the analogy breaks: a SLURM array task that fails just shows up as failed in `sacct`; a ReplicaSet actively *recreates* the failed Pod to restore the count, so 'how many ran' and 'how many are running' are different questions in K8s in a way they aren't in a one-shot array.

concept_map.slurm_analogy: A stable hostname/alias for a service that may move between nodes — like having a fixed DNS name that always points at 'whatever node is currently running the parameter server,' instead of hardcoding the compute node's hostname you got from `squeue`.
concept_map.explanation: Pods are mortal and get new IPs every restart, so you never address a Pod directly. A Service is a stable virtual IP (the ClusterIP) plus a DNS name that load-balances across all Pods matching its label selector. KubeDNS/CoreDNS resolves `gym-resources-svc` (or fully-qualified `gym-resources-svc.gym.svc.cluster.local`) to that virtual IP. THIS PR is built entirely on this: the agent reaches the model via `MODEL_HOST=gym-model-svc`, the model reaches the agent via `gym-agent-svc`, and the resources worker reaches both via Service names (see the env blocks). The Service's selector (`app.kubernetes.io/name: gym-resources`) is how it finds its backing Pods. Where it breaks vs SLURM: there's no SLURM equivalent of a built-in virtual-IP load balancer with DNS — you'd normally pass the node list around manually or use a side-channel. Finding F1 matters here: all this Service traffic is plaintext HTTP, despite the README's 'mTLS' claim. Finding F17 matters too: the *base* Service selectors are name-only, so if you ever applied base alongside an overlay in one namespace, the unprefixed Service could cross-select the overlay's Pods.

concept_map.slurm_analogy: A SLURM partition combined with an account/project scope — a named, quota-able, access-controlled slice of the cluster where your objects live and are addressed.
concept_map.explanation: A Namespace is a virtual cluster-within-the-cluster: a naming and isolation boundary. Object names must be unique *within* a namespace (two namespaces can each have a `gym-config`). DNS is namespace-aware (`svc.gym.svc.cluster.local`), RBAC and resource quotas are typically scoped per-namespace. THIS PR puts everything in namespace `gym` and runs *multiple benchmark instances in that one namespace*, relying on `namePrefix` (cg-, estc-) rather than separate namespaces to keep them apart. Where it breaks: a SLURM partition is mainly about node sets and scheduling policy; a Namespace is purely a logical/API boundary and does NOT by itself isolate the network. That distinction is the heart of finding F16 — there's no NetworkPolicy, so being in the same namespace gives naming isolation but NOT network isolation; a malicious code-gen Pod could still reach another instance's Ray GCS or the credentialed model service over the flat Pod network.

concept_map.slurm_analogy: A shared, version-controlled config file (or a block of `export VAR=...` lines) that you stage onto the node and your job reads — except it's a first-class cluster object, not a file on a shared filesystem.
concept_map.explanation: A ConfigMap stores non-secret configuration as key/value data, injected into Pods either as env vars or mounted as files. THIS PR's `gym-config` ConfigMap holds the entire Hydra config as one `config.yaml` key, and every server reads it via `NEMO_GYM_CONFIG_DICT` (env var sourced from `configMapKeyRef`). The config uses `${oc.env:...}` interpolation so the same ConfigMap works across servers — each server picks its slice with `NEMO_GYM_CONFIG_PATH` (agent uses `simple_agent_instance`, model uses `policy_model`). Where it breaks vs SLURM: editing a ConfigMap does NOT automatically restart consumers — Pods that read it as env vars keep the old values until recreated (similar to how exporting a new value doesn't change an already-running job, but here the config object updated silently and nothing rolled). Finding F19 is relevant: when a server boots from `NEMO_GYM_CONFIG_DICT`, the config-ref validation that `ng_run` normally does is skipped, so a dangling `resources_instance` reference only fails at request time, not boot time.

concept_map.slurm_analogy: The same idea as a ConfigMap but for credentials — like a locked-down file with 0600 perms holding your API token that the job sources, except managed by the cluster API with (optional) at-rest encryption and tighter RBAC.
concept_map.explanation: A Secret is base64-encoded key/value data intended for sensitive values, with stricter access controls than a ConfigMap (and can be encrypted at rest). THIS PR's `gym-secrets` holds `POLICY_BASE_URL`, `POLICY_API_KEY`, `POLICY_MODEL_NAME` — the external LLM credentials — and is injected via `envFrom: secretRef` into the agent, model, and resources containers. The secret.yaml is created per-overlay from secret.yaml.example and is intentionally NOT checked in. Where it breaks vs SLURM: base64 is encoding, not encryption — a Secret is only 'secret' relative to RBAC and at-rest config, not inherently. And finding F27 is a concrete gotcha: the README's troubleshooting `curl` command references `$POLICY_API_KEY` without an in-pod shell wrapper, so your LOCAL shell expands it to empty before kubectl runs — the secret lives only inside the Pod's env, so the documented diagnostic always fails.

concept_map.slurm_analogy: Your SLURM user/account identity plus the sacctmgr-defined permissions on what you may submit and where — but here it's an identity assigned to the *workload itself*, governing what the Pod's code may ask the cluster's control plane to do.
concept_map.explanation: A ServiceAccount is the identity a Pod runs as when it talks to the Kubernetes API server. RBAC (Roles + RoleBindings) grants that identity specific verbs on specific resource types. THIS PR uses a `gym-sa` ServiceAccount on all Pods. Crucially, the stateless Deployments set `automountServiceAccountToken: false` — they never call the K8s API, so they get no token (least privilege). The Ray Pods DO keep the token because the KubeRay autoscaler needs to call the API to create/delete worker Pods when scaling. Where it breaks vs SLURM: a SLURM job's identity governs *scheduling and accounting*; a ServiceAccount governs *control-plane API calls made by the running workload* — a category SLURM doesn't really have, since SLURM jobs don't reshape the cluster as they run. The autoscaler RBAC here was independently judged sound in the findings.

concept_map.slurm_analogy: One CLI that fuses sbatch (submit/apply), srun (exec into a running task), squeue/sacct (get/describe/list state), and scancel (delete) — all driven by declarative YAML rather than imperative submission.
concept_map.explanation: `kubectl` is the client to the cluster's API server. Rough mapping: `kubectl apply -f` ≈ `sbatch` (but declarative — you describe desired state and the cluster reconciles); `kubectl get pods` / `kubectl describe` ≈ `squeue` / `sacct`; `kubectl exec -it pod -- bash` ≈ `srun --pty` / sshing into the allocated node; `kubectl delete` ≈ `scancel`; `kubectl logs` ≈ tailing the job's stdout file. THIS PR's whole workflow is `kustomize build overlays/code-gen | kubectl apply -f -`. Where it breaks: `sbatch` returns a job ID for a thing that will end; `kubectl apply` registers desired state for things that persist and self-heal. Finding F5 is a kubectl-logs gotcha: the resources FastAPI server logs to `/tmp/nemo-gym-server.log`, not stdout, so `kubectl logs` on that Pod shows raylet output instead of the server — you must `kubectl exec ... cat` the file (and the CI 'Resources Logs' step is mislabeled because of this).

concept_map.slurm_analogy: Health checks SLURM mostly lacks: a recurring 'is this still alive?' watchdog (liveness ≈ a node health-check script that drains/reboots a bad node), a 'is it ready for traffic yet?' gate (readiness — no SLURM analog, since SLURM has no load balancer to gate), and a 'still booting, don't judge it yet' grace window (startup ≈ a prolog that must finish before the step counts as up).
concept_map.explanation: Three distinct kubelet-run checks. Startup probe: runs first, gives a slow-booting container a long grace window before the other probes apply (here, resources allows ~330s: failureThreshold 60 × period 5s + initialDelay 30s). Readiness probe: when it fails, the Pod is removed from its Service's endpoints — traffic is de-routed but the Pod is NOT killed. Liveness probe: when it fails, the kubelet RESTARTS the container. THIS PR points all three at `path: /docs` (the FastAPI Swagger page). Finding F3: `/docs` returns 200 as soon as uvicorn accepts a socket, so it's a shallow signal that can keep a wedged-but-listening server 'healthy.' Finding F4 (the sharpest one): on the resources Pod, liveness on `:9080` targets the FastAPI child, but PID 1 is the raylet — so a liveness failure restarts the WHOLE container, killing the raylet, not just the wedged child. Readiness would have de-routed it without the bounce.

concept_map.k8s_concept: requests vs limits (vs SLURM resource requests)
concept_map.slurm_analogy: `requests` ≈ `--mem`/`--cpus-per-task` used for *scheduling/placement* (the guaranteed reservation); `limits` ≈ a cgroup hard cap that SLURM can also enforce — but K8s lets request < limit, creating a 'burstable' middle zone SLURM's single number doesn't have.
concept_map.explanation: `requests` are what the scheduler uses to place a Pod: it only lands on a node with that much CPU/memory free, and that amount is guaranteed. `limits` are the hard ceiling enforced at runtime: exceed the memory limit and the container is OOM-killed; exceed the CPU limit and it's throttled (not killed). When request < limit, the Pod is 'Burstable' — it can use spare capacity up to the limit but is the first to be evicted under node pressure. THIS PR: the `gym-workers` request cpu:2/mem:4Gi and limit cpu:4/mem:8Gi (room to burst for test execution); `gym-resources` requests cpu:500m but runs `num-cpus:0` in Ray so it schedules no Ray compute. Where it breaks vs SLURM: SLURM typically reserves exactly what you asked and that's both the guarantee and the cap; K8s deliberately separates 'what's guaranteed' from 'what's allowed,' which enables overcommit but also surprise OOM-kills and evictions that have no clean SLURM analog. Finding F15 notes the gym-workers' cpu:2 request is why CI can't verify autoscaler scale-up on a single-node kind runner — four such workers won't fit.

concept_map.slurm_analogy: Job-script templating: a canonical `template.sbatch` (base) plus per-experiment override fragments (overlays) and a prefix on every job/output name to keep concurrent experiments from clobbering each other — but done by structured YAML merging, not envsubst/sed.
concept_map.explanation: Kustomize layers YAML without templating placeholders. The `base/` holds platform-agnostic manifests; an `overlay/` references the base and applies patches, a `namePrefix`, extra labels, image overrides, and `replacements`. THIS PR's `code-gen` overlay sets `namePrefix: cg-` (so `gym-agent` becomes `cg-gym-agent`), injects `app.kubernetes.io/instance: cg` into selectors and templates, patches the resources worker's entrypoint, and uses `replacements` to rewrite Service-name env vars to the prefixed names. This is what lets two benchmarks coexist in one namespace. Where it breaks vs sed-templating: Kustomize merges by structured paths, so it's more robust than text substitution — BUT finding F7 shows the limit: the RayCluster CRD has no merge keys, so the env patches address vars by absolute *array index* (`env/2`, `env/4`). Reorder the base env list and you silently mis-patch. Finding F9/F28 also live here: base pins `:latest` and no overlay sets an `images:` digest, so re-pushes don't trigger rollouts and pre-merge deployers hit ImagePullBackOff.

concept_map.slurm_analogy: Standing up a Ray cluster the way you would under SLURM with a head-node job + worker-node jobs sharing an address — but here a Kubernetes *operator* (the KubeRay controller) owns the lifecycle and can grow/shrink the worker pool automatically, like an elastic `salloc` that adds nodes on demand.
concept_map.explanation: RayCluster is a Custom Resource (a CRD KubeRay installs); the KubeRay operator watches it and creates the head Pod and worker-group Pods. THIS PR declares one head group (`num-cpus:0`, so it schedules no tasks — it's GCS/dashboard/coordination only) and two worker groups: `gym-workers` (num-cpus:2, the actual code-execution muscle, autoscaling 1→4) and `gym-resources` (num-cpus:0, max 1 — it hosts the FastAPI resources server and dispatches `.remote()` tasks but runs none itself). `enableInTreeAutoscaling: true` makes KubeRay inject an autoscaler sidecar into the head Pod (which is why the head is 2/2, not 3/3 — finding F26). Where it breaks vs a hand-rolled SLURM Ray cluster: under SLURM you'd statically allocate worker nodes for the job's lifetime; KubeRay's autoscaler adds/removes worker Pods dynamically based on pending Ray tasks, calling the K8s API to do it. Finding F15: CI never deploys a Ray-*using* overlay, so cross-node `.remote()` dispatch from gym-resources onto gym-workers is exercised at runtime but not asserted by any test.

concept_map.slurm_analogy: Running your job as an unprivileged user on a node where `/` is mounted read-only and only your scratch dir is writable — like a hardened compute node where you can't write outside `$TMPDIR` and can't escalate to root.
concept_map.explanation: A securityContext hardens the container: `runAsNonRoot: true` refuses to start as UID 0; `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false` blocks setuid escalation; `capabilities.drop: [ALL]` strips Linux capabilities; `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` makes the entire container filesystem read-only except explicitly mounted writable volumes; `seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault` applies the default syscall filter. THIS PR applies all of these to every container and mounts an `emptyDir` at `/tmp` as the only writable path. This matters because the gym-workers run *untrusted candidate code* from code-gen benchmarks. Where it breaks vs SLURM: SLURM relies on the node OS and your user account for isolation; here it's per-container kernel-level confinement. Two findings interact with read-only root: F8 (CPython can't write `__pycache__` next to the editable-installed source, so it silently recompiles bytecode every cold start — harmless but wasteful) and F21 (the `/etc/passwd` arbitrary-UID append in entrypoint.sh is a no-op because root is read-only, so it never fires).

concept_map.slurm_analogy: A task prolog / a backgrounded setup step that runs right after the main process launches — like an `srun` step that, once the node is up, forks a secondary daemon before the real work, except it runs in the same container as PID 1.
concept_map.explanation: `lifecycle.postStart` is a hook the kubelet runs immediately after a container's main process starts. It runs in the same container. THIS PR uses it for the cleverest and riskiest piece of the design: in the `gym-resources` worker, PID 1 is `ray start --block` (the raylet), and the postStart hook backgrounds a loop that waits for the raylet socket to appear, then `exec`s the NeMo Gym resources FastAPI server. This is how one Pod becomes both a Ray node AND the resources HTTP server. Where it breaks / risks: postStart runs concurrently with the main process and its failure handling is weak. Finding F6: the socket-wait `until` loop is unbounded and backgrounded, so postStart returns immediately and the Pod reports Running even if the socket never appears (the ~330s startup probe is the only backstop). Finding F4 again: because the FastAPI server is a detached child with no in-place supervisor, if it wedges, recovery is the heavyweight Pod restart, not a child restart.

concept_map.slurm_analogy: Running on a cluster that, for security, assigns your job a RANDOM unprivileged UID at launch (not your usual uid) — so any script that assumes a known username or writes to a fixed home dir must cope with 'I don't know who I am.'
concept_map.explanation: OpenShift's default SCC runs containers as a random high UID not present in the image's /etc/passwd. Software that calls getpwuid (to find a username/home) can fail. The standard mitigation is to make the relevant dirs group-writable (`chmod g=u`) and group-owned by root (GID 0), since the random UID always has GID 0, and to set HOME explicitly. THIS PR ships an `openshift` overlay (a Kustomize Component that sets `imagePullPolicy: Always`) and the image's entrypoint.sh attempts an /etc/passwd nss fallback. Where it breaks: finding F21 shows the /etc/passwd append is inert under `readOnlyRootFilesystem` (can't write /etc/passwd), so the load-bearing OpenShift hardening is actually the chgrp/chmod on /opt/app-root plus ENV HOME — the passwd branch never fires in the shipped config. There's no SLURM analog for 'the platform gives my process an identity that doesn't exist in any user database.'

concept_map.k8s_concept: port-forward (vs ssh tunnel to a compute node)
concept_map.slurm_analogy: Almost exactly an `ssh -L 8080:localhost:8080 compute-node` tunnel to reach a service bound on an allocated node — but it tunnels through the K8s API server to a Pod/Service, with no SSH daemon on the target and no inbound firewall opening.
concept_map.explanation: `kubectl port-forward svc/cg-gym-agent-svc 8080:8080` opens a local socket that forwards through the API server to the target Pod/Service. THIS PR deliberately exposes NOTHING externally (no LoadBalancer/Ingress); the README is explicit that all access — the agent API, the Ray dashboard on 8265, the head server on 11000 — is via port-forward only. This is the documented, correct security posture. Where it breaks vs ssh tunnel: there's no shell or sshd involved, it's an API-server-mediated stream, and it dies when your kubectl process exits; it's strictly for ad-hoc/dev access, not production traffic. This is also why finding F18 matters: you port-forward to the head server to run CLI tools like `ng_collect_rollouts`, but `ng_status` against the cluster always reports 'no servers found' because the standalone HeadServer never registers instances — so the analogy of 'tunnel in and inspect' partly fails for that one command.

teaching.architecture_walkthrough:
Here is one full request traced through the code-gen overlay (namePrefix `cg-`), naming every Pod and Service it touches. Think of it as one `srun` of the eval pipeline, except every hop is an HTTP call to a Service DNS name resolved by CoreDNS, and the 'compute' at the end is a Ray `.remote()` dispatch.

PARTICIPANTS (all in namespace `gym`):
- Deployment `cg-gym-agent` -> Pod `cg-gym-agent-*` (simple_agent FastAPI on :8080), fronted by Service `cg-gym-agent-svc`.
- Deployment `cg-gym-model` -> Pod `cg-gym-model-*` (vllm_model proxy on :8080), fronted by Service `cg-gym-model-svc`.
- RayCluster `cg-gym-ray` -> head Pod `cg-gym-ray-head-*` (ray-head + autoscaler sidecar = 2/2; GCS:6379, dashboard:8265, client:10001, num-cpus:0); worker group `gym-workers` Pods `cg-gym-ray-gym-workers-worker-*` (num-cpus:2, the code executors, autoscale 1->4); worker group `gym-resources` Pod `cg-gym-ray-gym-resources-worker-*` (num-cpus:0; PID1=raylet, postStart child=resources FastAPI on :9080), fronted by Service `cg-gym-resources-svc`.
- Deployment `cg-gym-head` -> Pod `cg-gym-head-*` (HeadServer config endpoint :11000), fronted by Service `cg-gym-head-svc`. The head is OUT of the request path — it only serves config to local CLI tools via port-forward.

STEP 0 (entry). A developer runs `kubectl port-forward svc/cg-gym-agent-svc 8080:8080` (the ssh-tunnel analog) and POSTs a task to `http://localhost:8080/run`. The forward lands on Service `cg-gym-agent-svc`, which load-balances (here, trivially, replicas:1) to the `cg-gym-agent-*` Pod. (In a rollout collection, the local `ng_collect_rollouts` does this same /run call.)

STEP 1 (agent -> model). simple_agent in `cg-gym-agent-*` runs its harness loop. It first seeds session state against the resources server, then calls the model. It resolves `MODEL_HOST=cg-gym-model-svc` (an env var that Kustomize `replacements` rewrote from the base `gym-model-svc` to the prefixed name) via CoreDNS to the model Service's ClusterIP, and POSTs to the model server's responses/chat endpoint on :8080. This is plaintext HTTP (finding F1 — there is no mTLS despite the README's claim).

STEP 2 (model -> external LLM). The `cg-gym-model-*` Pod is a vllm_model *proxy*, not a GPU server. It reads `POLICY_BASE_URL` / `POLICY_API_KEY` / `POLICY_MODEL_NAME` from the `cg-gym-secrets` Secret (via envFrom) and makes an outbound HTTPS call to the external OpenAI-compatible endpoint. `return_token_id_information: false` and `NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES: void` confirm this is eval-against-hosted-API only, no in-cluster inference or training token-id capture (finding C3). The LLM's completion flows back: external LLM -> model Pod -> agent Pod.

STEP 3 (agent extracts). Back in `cg-gym-agent-*`, simple_agent parses the model's response and extracts the candidate code / final answer from the trajectory.

STEP 4 (agent -> resources /verify). The agent resolves `RESOURCES_HOST=cg-gym-resources-svc` via CoreDNS and POSTs the candidate to the resources server's verify endpoint on :9080. Note what this Service points at: NOT a Deployment, but the `gym-resources` *Ray worker Pod*. Inside that Pod the request is served by the FastAPI process that the postStart hook launched after the raylet socket appeared (the F4/F6 mechanism). Note the agent Pod itself has `NEMO_GYM_SKIP_RAY_INIT=1` and `automountServiceAccountToken: false` — it never joins Ray and never calls the K8s API.

STEP 5 (resources -> Ray dispatch). The resources server's verify() for code-gen calls Ray. It is connected to the cluster as a Ray *client* (ray_head_node_address: "auto", joined to `cg-gym-ray`), but with num-cpus:0 it schedules nothing locally — so its `check_correctness_remote.remote()` calls dispatch the unit-test execution onto the `gym-workers` Pods (num-cpus:2 each), the only group with schedulable CPU. If demand exceeds capacity, the KubeRay autoscaler (sidecar in the head Pod) calls the K8s API to add gym-workers Pods up to maxReplicas:4. This cross-node dispatch (resources Pod -> head GCS -> gym-workers Pod) is the PR's central value and the slice CI does not assert (finding F15).

STEP 6 (run tests -> reward).
