Separately, the Deployments declare a coherent zero-downtime RollingUpdate (maxUnavailable:0/maxSurge:1, minReadySeconds:10, preStop sleep 5, terminationGracePeriodSeconds 30), but the RayCluster is a KubeRay CRD and does NOT honor Deployment-style rollingUpdate — its pod replacement follows KubeRay's reconcile semantics, so the resources/worker pods do not get the same zero-downtime guarantees.
finding.evidence: raycluster.yaml:56-61 ray-head cpu request only (no cpu limit); gym-workers raycluster.yaml:102-108 req cpu 2 / limit cpu 4. Deployments declare RollingUpdate maxUnavailable:0/maxSurge:1; RayCluster has no such field.
finding.recommendation: No change required if Burstable is intended; confirm intent and that no namespace LimitRange overrides it. Document that RayCluster pods are upgraded via KubeRay's reconcile (not Deployment rollingUpdate) so operators don't expect maxUnavailable:0 semantics there.

devil.challenge: See challenge field.
devil.strongest_counterargument: The finding is not technically WRONG — every fact it states is verifiable in the manifests, and it is honest enough to self-rate as nit and label the CPU choice defensible. Its surviving value is the genuinely useful documentation observation that operators should not expect Deployment maxUnavailable:0 zero-downtime semantics to apply to the RayCluster's head/resources pods (which are upgraded via KubeRay reconcile, potentially with a service gap). That is a real, easy-to-miss operational gotcha worth a one-line doc note, so the finding is accurate-but-low-value rather than false. Whether to keep it is a judgment call about signal-to-noise, not about correctness.
points_to_verify: Confirm k8s/base/ ships no LimitRange or namespace resource that would inject CPU limit defaults (kustomize build k8s/base shows only SA, ConfigMap, RayCluster, Deployments, Services) — if none, the LimitRange caveat is purely hypothetical and not actionable in this PR.

Confirm raycluster.yaml contains no spec.headGroupSpec/workerGroupSpecs rollingUpdate or upgrade-strategy field, so the manifests make no false zero-downtime promise (the RollingUpdate inconsistency is between separate APIs, by design).

Decide whether a self-rated nit that recommends only 'confirm intent' and 'document' — with both halves explicitly calling the author's choice defensible — meets the bar to be reported at all under the 'do not pad' rule.

Verify the head Deployment is a single-replica stateless config-server (deployment-head.yaml replicas:1) and that ray-head is inherently stateful, so 'same zero-downtime guarantees' for those pods is not an achievable or expected target — weakening the inconsistency framing.

adjudication.reasoning:
Every factual claim in the finding is verifiable in the manifests. raycluster.yaml: ray-head has cpu request 500m (line 58) + memory request 2Gi (59) but only a memory limit 8Gi (61), no CPU limit -> Burstable QoS (CONFIRMED). gym-resources worker has cpu request 500m / mem 512Mi (192-193), only memory limit 2Gi (195), no CPU limit (CONFIRMED). gym-workers is the only container with both cpu request 2 (104) and cpu limit 4 (107) (CONFIRMED) -- appropriate since ray-remote compute lands there. deployment-agent/model both have cpu request 250m and only a memory limit (1Gi/agent line 73), no CPU limit -> Burstable (CONFIRMED). deployment-head has cpu request 100m, memory limit 512Mi, no CPU limit, replicas:1 (CONFIRMED). All three Deployments declare RollingUpdate maxUnavailable:0/maxSurge:1, minReadySeconds:10, terminationGracePeriodSeconds:30, preStop sleep 5 (CONFIRMED for head/agent/model). The RayCluster headGroupSpec and workerGroupSpecs contain no rollingUpdate/upgrade-strategy field (full file read).

Now the devil's four points, all of which check out: (1) `kubectl kustomize k8s/base` renders exactly ConfigMap, 3 Deployments, 1 RayCluster, 4 Services, 1 ServiceAccount -- NO LimitRange and no Namespace object. The LimitRange-injecting-CPU-defaults caveat is purely hypothetical and not actionable in this PR. (2) Since KubeRay's CRD has no rollingUpdate field, the manifests make no false zero-downtime promise; the so-called inconsistency is simply a comparison across two different APIs and is by design. (3) The finding self-rates nit, explicitly calls the CPU/Burstable choice 'defensible,' and recommends only 'confirm intent' / 'document' with 'no change required if Burstable is intended.' (4) head is a single-replica stateless config server and ray-head is inherently stateful, so 'same zero-downtime guarantees' for those pods is neither achievable nor an expected target -- the inconsistency framing overstates a non-problem.

Net: the finding is accurate-but-low-value, not wrong. The CPU/Burstable observation is correct and self-described as defensible with no change required. The LimitRange caveat is unfounded against this PR (none ships). The only durable, non-trivial nugget is a one-line operator doc note that RayCluster head/resources pods are reconciled by KubeRay, not by Deployment maxUnavailable:0 semantics, so operators should not expect identical zero-downtime behavior there. That is genuine but minor. I keep the finding at nit and trim its scope to that single doc note; I reject the LimitRange caveat and the framing that the QoS choice or the cross-API rolling-update difference is a defect.
adjudication.final_recommendation: No code change required. The Burstable QoS (CPU request without CPU limit) is a deliberate, defensible choice for bursty FastAPI/Ray work and avoids CFS throttling; gym-workers correctly carries both cpu req 2/limit 4 since ray-remote compute lands there. Drop the LimitRange caveat -- `kubectl kustomize k8s/base` ships no LimitRange or Namespace object, so it is hypothetical and not actionable in this PR. The cross-API rolling-update difference is not a defect: KubeRay's RayCluster CRD has no rollingUpdate field, so the manifests make no false zero-downtime promise. The single durable, low-value action is an optional one-line doc note (e.g. in a k8s README) clarifying that RayCluster head/resources pods are replaced via KubeRay reconcile rather than the Deployment-style maxUnavailable:0 semantics used for the agent/model/head Deployments, so operators don't assume identical zero-downtime guarantees for the Ray pods.

finding.title: New k8s files lack SPDX headers
finding.description: 21 of 22 new files miss SPDX; only mock_model has it. Not in F1-F32.
finding.evidence: grep flags all but mock_model
finding.recommendation: Add SPDX headers.

devil.challenge:
The finding overstates a real-but-minor issue and gets its own facts wrong, but it is not fully a non-issue. Strongest case against it:

1) FACTUAL ERROR IN THE FINDING ITSELF. The claim "21 of 22 new files miss SPDX" is wrong. `grep -rL SPDX /tmp/pr-1231-worktree/k8s/` shows ~30 files lack SPDX and only `k8s/tests/mock_model.py` has it. A finding that miscounts its own evidence is weakened; the count should not be trusted as written.

2) THE REPO DOES NOT REQUIRE SPDX ON THE FILE TYPES THE PR AUTHOR OMITTED. The k8s subtree is almost entirely YAML (Kustomize manifests, configmaps, services, raycluster), Dockerfiles/Containerfiles, shell, and Markdown. The existing repo's own YAML config `resources_servers/example_single_tool_call/configs/example_single_tool_call.yaml` has NO SPDX header (verified: first line is `example_single_tool_call:`). So NeMo Gym demonstrably does NOT enforce headers on YAML. Flagging the k8s YAML/configmap/service/raycluster files for missing SPDX holds them to a stricter standard than the repo applies to itself — directly violating the task's own rule about not judging against unrelated conventions the repo doesn't follow.

3) THE AUTHOR KNEW AND FOLLOWED THE PYTHON CONVENTION. The only Python source file in the k8s subtree that is a substantive program, `k8s/tests/mock_model.py`, carries the full SPDX+Apache header verbatim matching `nemo_gym/server_utils.py`. This shows a deliberate, correct application of the convention where it actually applies (.py files). `k8s/head/entrypoint.py` (the one remaining .py, a 312-byte trivial config-serving stub) is the only genuinely defensible miss, and it is a single file — nowhere near "21."

4) NOT A DEPLOY/RUNTIME/SECURITY ISSUE. Missing license headers do not break the deploy, cause runtime failure, or open a security hole. Per the severity rubric, "medium" is reserved for a "real correctness/maintainability problem." A missing comment header on declarative YAML is at most nit/low for maintainability; it has zero functional impact. The severity is inflated.

5) THE CI TEMPLATE, NOT THE REVIEWER, IS THE ENFORCER. `.github/workflows/copyright-check.yml` runs on every PR via the external FW-CI-templates `_copyright_check.yml@v0.55.0`. If that check requires headers on these files, CI will block the merge automatically and tell the author exactly which files — making a manual review finding redundant. If it does NOT match these file types (plausible, since the repo's own YAML lacks headers), then there is no policy to violate at all. Either way the standalone review finding adds little.

Net: the underlying observation (some new files lack SPDX headers) is technically true, but the finding is overstated in count and severity, and largely targets file types the repo itself does not header. The only clean, defensible instance is `k8s/head/entrypoint.py`.
devil.strongest_counterargument: The repo DOES run a copyright-check CI job on every PR (`.github/workflows/copyright-check.yml`). If the external FW-CI-templates `_copyright_check.yml@v0.55.0` matches YAML/Dockerfile/shell (many NVIDIA copyright checkers do match `*.yaml`, `Dockerfile`, and `*.sh`), then ALL these missing-header files would fail CI and block the merge — which would make the finding correct and arguably under-rated rather than over-rated. The PR author also clearly knows the convention (mock_model.py has the header), so the omission across 30 files is a genuine oversight that an enforced check would catch. This is why I cannot push the probability below ~0.5: the existence of an active copyright-check workflow is strong evidence the policy is real and enforced repo-wide, and the exact match scope of the external template is the single fact that decides whether this is a blocker (finding correct) or a non-issue (finding wrong).
points_to_verify: Fetch NVIDIA-NeMo/FW-CI-templates/.github/workflows/_copyright_check.yml@v0.55.0 and read its file-extension/path match list — does it match *.yaml, Dockerfile/Containerfile, *.sh, *.md? This single fact decides whether the finding is a CI-blocking issue or a non-issue.

Confirm the actual count: `grep -rL SPDX /tmp/pr-1231-worktree/k8s/` returns ~30 files missing SPDX and only k8s/tests/mock_model.py has it — the finding's '21 of 22' is inaccurate and should be corrected.

Confirm existing repo YAML configs lack SPDX headers (e.g. resources_servers/example_single_tool_call/configs/example_single_tool_call.yaml starts with `example_single_tool_call:`), establishing the repo does not require headers on YAML.
Verify whether the copyright-check template excludes generated/declarative manifests or has an allowlist (some templates skip non-source files), which would narrow the real violation set.
Check the diff/CI status of PR #1231 itself: did the copyright-check job pass or fail? Actual CI result is the ground truth on whether these files violate policy.

adjudication.final_recommendation: Add the NVIDIA Apache-2.0 SPDX header to k8s/head/entrypoint.py, the only file CI copyright-check will flag since it matches .py only and mock_model.py already has it. Do not require SPDX on the YAML manifests, Dockerfile, Containerfile, sh, or md files: CI does not check them and the repo ships YAML without headers. Optional comment headers on Dockerfile and entrypoint.sh are a nit. Correct the count to one enforced .py miss out of 30 total unheadered files.
adjudication.reasoning:
Finding is true at its core but wrong on facts and overstated. (1) Count: grep -rL SPDX over the k8s worktree shows 30 files missing SPDX, only mock_model.py has it, not 21 of 22. (2) CI template _copyright_check.yml v0.55.0 matches only .py by default (find -name star.py with additional-find-args optional); YAML, Dockerfile, sh, md are not checked unless additional-find-args is passed. (3) The local caller copyright-check.yml uses the template with no with block, so additional-find-args is empty and only .py is checked, refuting the devil's strongest counterargument that the template might match YAML or Dockerfile. (4) The repo itself ships YAML without headers (example_single_tool_call.yaml line 1 is example_single_tool_call colon), so flagging k8s YAML and manifests is stricter than the repo applies to itself. (5) Of the two .py files in k8s, mock_model.py has the header and k8s/head/entrypoint.py does not; neither is an init file, so CI flags exactly one file: k8s/head/entrypoint.py. Devil correct on points 1 to 5; finding correct that a header is missing but overstated in count, scope, and severity.</parameter>
<parameter name="evidence_examined">k8s worktree SPDX grep (30 missing, only mock_model.py present); copyright-check.yml full read (template v0.55.0, no with block); external _copyright_check.yml v0.55.0 via WebFetch (matches .py, additional-find-args optional, excludes init files); example_single_tool_call.yaml line 1 has no header.

finding.title: No PVC; outputs on tmp emptyDir lost on restart
finding.description: No PVC; readOnly root, only tmp emptyDir; rollouts vanish. F31 is tmp-sizing.
finding.evidence: no PVC in k8s
finding.recommendation: Add PVC or note ephemerality.

devil.challenge: The finding's core premise — that rollout "outputs" live on a pod tmp emptyDir and "vanish on restart" — is wrong for how this deployment actually works. In NeMo Gym's k8s topology, the deployed pods (resources/agent/model) are stateless HTTP services; they never write rollout artifacts to disk. Rollouts are produced by `ng_collect_rollouts` running on the OPERATOR's local machine via `kubectl port-forward`, writing to a client-side path. The README's own example (k8s/README.md:153-158) proves this: `ng_collect_rollouts ... +output_jsonl_fpath=/tmp/rollouts.jsonl` — that `/tmp` is the local CLI host, not a pod. So there is no rollout data inside any pod that could be lost on restart. The only contents of the pod emptyDirs are genuinely-ephemeral runtime scratch: Ray session sockets/spill via RAY_TMPDIR=/tmp (raycluster.yaml:52-53,162-163) and the relaunchable server log /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log (raycluster.yaml:156). The `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` + writable `/tmp` emptyDir pairing (deployment-agent.yaml:99/104-109, raycluster.yaml:64/68-74) is the canonical, deliberate Kubernetes/OpenShift hardening pattern, not an oversight. Adding a PVC would be actively harmful: it conflicts with the autoscaling worker group (gym-workers scales 1→4, raycluster.yaml:77-79) and RWO single-attach semantics, and persists nothing of value for stateless servers. The README also effectively documents the ephemeral, interactive nature of the deployment (k8s/README.md:167 'No services are exposed externally — all access is via kubectl port-forward'). The recommendation 'Add PVC or note ephemerality' is largely already satisfied. The finding mistakes a stateless serving topology for a batch-results topology.
devil.strongest_counterargument: The finding survives in a narrow, weakened form: the PR never states explicitly 'pods are stateless; nothing persists; outputs are written client-side', so a reader could plausibly expect server-side persistence and be surprised. Concretely, the postStart server log redirected to /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log (raycluster.yaml:156) IS lost on pod restart, and since stdout is redirected to that file rather than the container's stdout, kubectl logs won't capture it either — a genuine (if minor) observability gap that complicates post-mortem debugging. If a user ever ran ng_reward_profile or wrote outputs to a path that happened to resolve inside a pod, those would indeed be ephemeral. So 'note ephemerality' has real (low-severity) merit even though 'Add PVC' does not. This is why I do not put the probability above ~0.7.
points_to_verify: Confirm rollout output path is client-side: k8s/README.md:153-158 shows ng_collect_rollouts runs locally via port-forward writing to local /tmp/rollouts.jsonl, not in any pod

Confirm resources/agent/model pods do not write rollout/result artifacts to /tmp — the emptyDirs only hold Ray scratch (RAY_TMPDIR=/tmp, raycluster.yaml:52-53,98-99,162-163) and the server log (raycluster.yaml:156)

Confirm readOnlyRootFilesystem:true + writable /tmp emptyDir is intentional hardening (deployment-agent.yaml:99 & 104-109; raycluster.yaml:64,111,217 with mounts) and that a PVC would conflict with the 1->4 autoscaling gym-workers group (raycluster.yaml:77-79)

Verify the genuine residual gap: /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log (raycluster.yaml:156) is redirected away from container stdout, so kubectl logs won't show server output and the log is lost on restart — assess whether this alone justifies keeping a low-severity 'observability/note-ephemerality' finding rather than the stated persistence/data-loss framing
Check k8s/README.md:167 and the overall CLI-driven workflow to confirm the deployment is documented as an interactive serving topology rather than a batch-results topology

adjudication.reasoning: See full reasoning above: the persistence/data-loss premise is refuted (no rollout artifacts in pods; outputs are client-side; readOnly root + /tmp emptyDir is intentional hardening; PVC would conflict with autoscaling worker group). Only a minor observability gap on /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log survives, which the README already partly mitigates.
adjudication.final_recommendation: Drop "Add PVC"; keep a low-severity observability note about /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log not appearing in kubectl logs and being lost on restart, and document that pods are stateless with outputs written client-side.
adjudication.evidence_examined: /tmp/pr-1231-worktree/k8s/base/raycluster.yaml (full, esp. lines 52-74 head /tmp + RAY_TMPDIR; 77-79 autoscaling gym-workers; 109-121 worker /tmp; 145-157 postStart relaunch with `>> /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log 2>&1`; 162-227 resources worker /tmp emptyDir + readOnlyRootFilesystem:true); /tmp/pr-1231-worktree/k8s/base/deployment-agent.yaml (lines 49-52 RAY_TMPDIR/SKIP_RAY_INIT, 99 readOnlyRootFilesystem, 104-109 /tmp emptyDir sizeLimit 1Gi — stateless serving); /tmp/pr-1231-worktree/k8s/base/deployment-model.yaml (identical stateless pattern, 99 readOnlyRootFilesystem, 104-109 /tmp emptyDir); /tmp/pr-1231-worktree/k8s/README.md (lines 26 data flow; 138-159 ng_collect_rollouts run client-side via port-forward writing /tmp/rollouts.jsonl on the local host; 167 no external services / all via port-forward; 363 troubleshooting cat /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log).

finding.title: Model is GPU-free proxy; no in-cluster vLLM
finding.description: NVIDIA void, no GPU resource, external URL; vLLM cannot run. F29 is generality.
finding.evidence: void x3; no GPU resource
finding.recommendation: Document external-only or add GPU overlay.

devil.challenge: The finding mischaracterizes a deliberate, explicitly-documented design choice as an incompleteness defect. (1) The PR never intends to host model weights in-cluster — the README architecture diagram (k8s/README.md:23) terminates at an "External LLM endpoint (OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions)" with an explicit HTTPS arrow leaving the cluster, and the data flow (line 26) reads "model /v1/responses -> external LLM". (2) The claim "vLLM cannot run" is factually wrong because it conflates NeMo Gym's `vllm_model` SERVER (an OpenAI-compatible client harness) with a vLLM inference ENGINE hosting weights. The `vllm_model` server runs perfectly fine as a thin proxy: configmap.yaml:33-41 configures it purely as a client (base_url/api_key/model all from POLICY_BASE_URL/POLICY_API_KEY/POLICY_MODEL_NAME env), and deployment-model.yaml:68-73 requests only 250m CPU / 512Mi — clearly a proxy, never a weight server. The server starts, serves /v1/responses, and forwards to the external endpoint; nothing "cannot run." (3) `NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES: void` is the correct, intentional sentinel telling the NVIDIA container runtime to inject zero GPUs — appropriate for CPU-only pods (Ray workers run unit tests; model is a proxy). It is consistently set on all three Ray containers, none of which need a GPU. It is not an accidental omission. (4) The recommendation "Document external-only OR add GPU overlay" is already mostly satisfied: external-only is documented exhaustively (arch diagram, data flow, POLICY_BASE_URL env table at README:106, deployment comment at README:46 'vllm_model proxy (skips Ray)', and a reachability troubleshooting curl at README:370). So this is, at most, a low/nit feature-scope observation that no self-contained GPU overlay ships — not a medium 'gpu' correctness gap. The PR's stated scope ("run the gym on Kubernetes" against an external LLM) is fully met.
devil.strongest_counterargument: The one residual point with merit: NeMo Gym's primary value proposition and CLAUDE.md guidance emphasize the Model Server + local vLLM for OPEN models and for training (token-id capture). This k8s subtree cannot serve open-weight models in-cluster and even disables token-id capture (configmap.yaml:42 `return_token_id_information: false`), so it is eval-against-hosted-API only and unusable for the training workflows the repo otherwise centers on. If the reviewer's bar is "should the headline k8s story support the library's flagship local-vLLM/training use case," then the absence of any GPU/in-cluster-inference path is a genuine generality gap worth flagging — which keeps a weakened version of the finding alive (though still better framed as scope/docs than a 'gpu' bug, and arguably out of scope for a first optional k8s contribution).
points_to_verify: Confirm NeMo Gym's responses_api_models/vllm_model/app.py is an OpenAI-compatible CLIENT that requires an external base_url and does NOT spawn a vLLM engine in-process (read responses_api_models/vllm_model/app.py on main) — this is the load-bearing fact that 'vLLM cannot run' is false because the proxy is exactly how vllm_model is designed to operate when base_url is set.

Verify NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=void is a recognized NVIDIA container runtime sentinel meaning 'inject no GPU devices' (not a typo/placeholder) and that none of the three Ray containers or the model deployment legitimately need a GPU for their workloads (Ray workers run unit tests; model proxies; resources verifies).

Confirm the README genuinely documents the external-only design as intentional rather than as a known limitation: lines 23, 26, 46, 101, 106, 244, 367-370 all describe configuring an external POLICY_BASE_URL endpoint.

Check whether the finding's severity ('medium', category 'gpu') is justified vs. the actual nature (feature-scope/docs observation). The deployment resource requests (250m CPU, 512Mi) prove no GPU was ever intended for the model pod.

Assess whether the orchestrator's bar for 'completeness' expects the k8s story to support the repo's flagship local-vLLM/training workflow; if not, the finding collapses to a nit. If yes, only a weakened scope-gap version survives.

adjudication.reasoning:
I verified each load-bearing fact against the actual files.

(1) vllm_model is a CLIENT, not an in-process engine — CONFIRMED. responses_api_models/vllm_model/app.py:121-129 (_post_init) builds `NeMoGymAsyncOpenAI(base_url=base_url, api_key=...)` per configured base_url and forwards via client.create_chat_completion / create_response. There is no vLLM engine spawned in-process. So the original finding's phrasing "vLLM cannot run" is MISLEADING: the vllm_model server runs perfectly fine as a thin proxy when base_url is set, which is exactly its design. configmap.yaml:33-46 wires it purely as a client (base_url/api_key/model from POLICY_BASE_URL/POLICY_API_KEY/POLICY_MODEL_NAME), and deployment-model.yaml:68-73 requests only 250m CPU / 512Mi with no GPU — a proxy, never a weight server. The devil is right on this point; the finding overstated it.

(2) NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES: void — CONFIRMED as an intentional, recognized NVIDIA Container Toolkit sentinel meaning "inject zero GPUs." raycluster.yaml:54-55, 100-101, 164-165 set it consistently on all three Ray containers, none of which run GPU workloads (Ray workers execute unit tests; resources verifies). It is not a typo/placeholder and not an accidental omission. The "gpu" category framing is therefore wrong — there is no GPU bug.

(3) External-only design IS documented as the intended architecture — CONFIRMED. k8s/README.md:23 ("External LLM endpoint (OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions)"), :26 data flow ("model /v1/responses -> external LLM"), :46 ("vllm_model proxy (skips Ray)"), :106 (POLICY_BASE_URL env table), :370 (reachability curl). The recommendation "document external-only" is largely satisfied.

(4) BUT the residual scope gap survives (the devil's own strongest counterargument). A scoped grep of k8s/README.md for "training|token.id|open.weight|in-cluster|limitation|GPU|eval.only" returned NOTHING. The README documents external-only as the intended design but NEVER acknowledges it as a limitation relative to NeMo Gym's flagship workflow. CLAUDE.md explicitly centers local vLLM for open-weight models and token-id capture for training ("manages token IDs needed for training"). This subtree disables that — configmap.yaml:42 `return_token_id_information: false` — and offers no in-cluster inference path, so it is eval-against-hosted-API only and unusable for the training workflows the repo centers on. That is a genuine, defensible scope/docs observation worth a brief note.

Net: the finding's underlying observation (no in-cluster GPU/vLLM inference; eval-against-external-API only; training/token-id workflow unsupported) is factually true, but the finding is mischaracterized. "vLLM cannot run" is wrong, the "gpu" category is wrong (void is correct-by-design), and the design intent is documented. So: partially_confirmed, downgraded from medium/gpu to low/docs-scope.
adjudication.final_recommendation: Reframe and downgrade. Drop the "gpu" framing and the "vLLM cannot run" claim — both are inaccurate (NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES: void is the correct sentinel for CPU-only pods, and vllm_model is by design an OpenAI-compatible client proxy that runs fine forwarding to POLICY_BASE_URL). Keep only the weakened, accurate version: add one short "Scope / Limitations" note to k8s/README.md stating that this deployment targets evaluation against an external OpenAI-compatible endpoint only — it does not host open-weight models in-cluster (no GPU path) and disables training token-id capture (configmap.yaml:42 return_token_id_information: false), so it does not cover NeMo Gym's local-vLLM/training workflow. Optionally note that a GPU-enabled in-cluster inference overlay is future work. This is a docs/scope nit appropriate for a first optional k8s contribution, not a blocking correctness defect.

finding.title: Images amd64-only; no multi-arch
finding.description: Only linux/amd64 in diff; no platforms key. Breaks ARM.
finding.evidence: only amd64 token in diff
finding.recommendation: buildx multi-arch.

devil.challenge:
The core technical claim is accurate — the build workflow (.github/workflows/build-k8s-images.yml lines 80-91, docker/build-push-action@v6) has NO `platforms:` key, so on ubuntu-latest runners buildx publishes amd64-only manifests. That part is verifiable and not a false positive. But the finding is overstated and arguably a non-issue in framing/severity:

1. "Breaks ARM" is misleading. The images do not break anything — they simply lack ARM manifests, so an ARM pull fails with no-matching-manifest. This is a missing optional feature, not a regression. Pre-PR there was zero k8s support, so no ARM capability is lost; the entire k8s/ subtree is greenfield, and it ADDS amd64 support where none existed.

2. The evidence string is imprecise. The ONLY literal `amd64` token in the diff is a documentation comment in k8s/head/Containerfile line 6 (`podman build --platform linux/amd64 ...`), an example local-build recipe — NOT the workflow. The actual load-bearing fact (absence of a `platforms:` key) is never quoted. The author's comment even shows a deliberate, documented amd64 target.

3. Deliberate, defensible scoping. Multi-arch via QEMU emulation greatly increases CI time/cost; shipping amd64-first (the dominant cloud/CI arch) for an initial enablement PR and adding `platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64` later is standard incremental practice.

4. The machinery is already present: both workflows use docker/setup-buildx-action@v3 (build line 58, integration-test line 35), so ARM is a one-line `platforms:` follow-up — not an architectural defect. The UBI9/UBI10 base images are themselves multi-arch, so there is no upstream blocker.

5. Severity is already correctly set to the lowest non-nit tier ("low"), so there is little practical impact to overstate.

Net: the technical observation is true, so I cannot call it a false positive; but the title/description ("Breaks ARM") overstate it and the evidence cites a comment rather than the real cause.
devil.strongest_counterargument: It is genuinely true and verifiable that the build pipeline emits amd64-only images (no `platforms:` key in build-push-action), and ARM clusters (e.g., AWS Graviton, increasingly common) cannot run these images at all — they fail to pull. Since the PR explicitly targets Kubernetes/cloud where ARM nodes are mainstream, the omission is a real, if low-severity, portability gap. The finding is therefore correct on the merits; only its framing ("Breaks ARM") is slightly dramatic. A correctly-rated low finding with accurate facts should not be dismissed.
points_to_verify: Confirm .github/workflows/build-k8s-images.yml lines 80-91 (docker/build-push-action@v6) contain no `platforms:` key, so default native amd64 build occurs on ubuntu-latest
Confirm the only literal 'amd64' string in the diff is the comment at k8s/head/Containerfile line 6, not an actual hard arch pin in the workflow
Verify UBI9/UBI10 python-312 base images publish multi-arch manifests (so the limitation is purely in the build pipeline, not the base layer)
Confirm both workflows already use docker/setup-buildx-action (build line 58, integration-test line 35), making a multi-arch addition a minimal change
Confirm there is no stated ARM-support requirement in the k8s/ subtree or PR README, and that pre-PR there was no k8s support at all (greenfield, nothing regressed)

adjudication.reasoning:
The underlying technical claim is real and verifiable: .github/workflows/build-k8s-images.yml lines 80-91 use docker/build-push-action@v6 with no `platforms:` key (only context/file/target/push/tags/labels/cache-from/cache-to). On ubuntu-latest, buildx defaults to the runner's native arch (amd64), so published GHCR manifests are amd64-only. An ARM node would fail to pull with "no matching manifest." So this is NOT a false positive — confirming the devil's own concession.

However, the finding's framing and evidence are inaccurate, which is why this is partial rather than full confirmation:
1. "Breaks ARM" overstates it. The entire k8s/ subtree is greenfield (pre-PR nemo_gym/server_utils.py had zero k8s support); nothing regressed. ARM manifests are simply absent, a missing optional feature, not a break.
2. The evidence string ("only amd64 token in diff") misattributes the cause. I confirmed via grep that the ONLY amd64/arm64 token in all of k8s/ is a documentation comment at k8s/head/Containerfile:6 (`podman build --platform linux/amd64 ...`), an example local-build recipe — NOT the workflow. The README.md "platform-agnostic" hits refer to Kustomize/OpenShift overlays, not CPU arch. The actual load-bearing fact (absent `platforms:` key) is never quoted by the finding.
3. Buildx machinery is present (setup-buildx-action@v3 at build line 58, integration-test line 35), so adding ARM is mostly a `platforms:` line — though note docker/setup-qemu-action is absent in both workflows, so true cross-arch emulation would also need QEMU setup, making it marginally more than a one-liner.
4. UBI9/UBI10 base images are multi-arch by Red Hat policy, so the limitation lives purely in the build pipeline, not the base layer.

Net: real low-severity portability gap, correctly rated low, but the title ("Breaks ARM") and evidence (pointing at the Containerfile comment instead of the missing `platforms:` key) are imprecise.
adjudication.evidence_examined: .github/workflows/build-k8s-images.yml (read full: lines 80-91 build-push-action@v6 has no platforms key; setup-buildx at line 58; runs-on ubuntu-latest line 33; no setup-qemu-action present). k8s/head/Containerfile (read full: only amd64 reference is the build-recipe comment at line 6). grep across both workflows for platforms/amd64/arm64/buildx/qemu — no platforms or amd64 token in either workflow, no setup-qemu-action anywhere. grep across all of k8s/ — sole amd64/arm64 token is Containerfile:6 comment; README.md hits are about Kustomize/OpenShift overlays not CPU arch. Compared to base /home/adasif/Desktop/Gym (no k8s subtree pre-PR — greenfield, nothing regressed).
adjudication.final_recommendation: Keep as a low-severity portability note, but correct the framing and evidence. Replace "Breaks ARM" with "Published images are amd64-only; ARM (e.g. Graviton) nodes fail to pull with no-matching-manifest." Cite the true cause: absence of a `platforms:` key in docker/build-push-action@v6 at build-k8s-images.yml lines 80-91 (not the Containerfile:6 comment). Recommended fix: add `platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64` to the build step AND add a `docker/setup-qemu-action@v3` step (currently missing in both workflows) to enable cross-arch emulation, since setup-buildx alone does not provide QEMU. UBI bases are already multi-arch, so no base-layer blocker. Acceptable to defer as an incremental follow-up given CI emulation cost.

finding.title: No image signing/SBOM/provenance
finding.description: No cosign/sbom/provenance tokens. Distinct from F9/F14.
finding.evidence: no such tokens in diff
finding.recommendation: Add signing, SBOM, provenance.

devil.challenge: The finding is factually accurate but overstated and arguably a non-actionable scope nit; the strongest case it is 'wrong' is the default-provenance behavior of build-push-action v6 plus its out-of-scope, low-value framing.
devil.strongest_counterargument: docker/build-push-action@v6 enables SLSA provenance attestations by default on registry pushes, so the categorical claim "no provenance tokens / no provenance" misstates the produced artifacts; combined with the finding being a self-rated "low" residual completeness wish for a first-cut optional subtree, it is overstated gold-plating rather than a real defect.
points_to_verify: Confirm docker/build-push-action@v6 default provenance behavior: does it actually attach SLSA provenance by default when push=true to GHCR, or is the default suppressed on GitHub Actions/GHCR contexts? (This determines whether 'no provenance' is literally false.)
Confirm there is no `provenance:` or `sbom:` key anywhere in build-k8s-images.yml (lines 80-90 show none) and no separate cosign/attestation step elsewhere in the workflow.
Confirm there is no other workflow file under .github/workflows/ in the PR (e.g. k8s-integration-test.yml) that adds signing/SBOM/provenance for these images.

Judge scope appropriateness: whether the project's other published Docker images / existing workflows sign or SBOM their artifacts (establishing a convention the PR diverges from) vs. this being novel optional infra with no such precedent.
Verify the images push to a contributor/repo-scoped GHCR path, not an official distribution channel, which lowers the supply-chain blast radius and supports 'low' severity.

adjudication.reasoning:
The finding's load-bearing claims are factually accurate. In /tmp/pr-1231-worktree/.github/workflows/build-k8s-images.yml the "Build and push" step (lines 80-91) has no `provenance:` key, no `sbom:` key, and no separate cosign/attestation step; a scoped grep for provenance|sbom|cosign|attest|sign across both new workflows returned exit 1 (zero matches). The PR adds only two workflows (build-k8s-images.yml, k8s-integration-test.yml); the integration-test workflow's four build steps all use `load: true` (no push, lines 44/55/66/77), so they neither publish nor sign anything. So "no SBOM, no signing, no explicit provenance" is literally true of the workflow source.

On the devil's strongest counterargument — that docker/build-push-action@v6 attaches SLSA provenance by default — the technical reality undercuts it: build-push-action disables provenance by default when it detects a GitHub Actions runner (GITHUB_ACTIONS=true) to avoid breaking registries, so the GHA default is effectively provenance=false unless set explicitly. Since no `provenance:` key is set here, the pushed images most likely carry no provenance attestation, and even any min-mode provenance would be unsigned (no cosign) with no SBOM. The categorical "no provenance" claim is therefore not refuted.

The devil is right on the framing/severity axis, but the finding already self-rates "low." I verified there is no project convention being violated: the pre-existing official image pipeline /home/adasif/Desktop/Gym/.github/workflows/_build_container.yml also contains no cosign/sbom/provenance/attest tokens (grep exit 1). The new images push to repo-scoped GHCR (ghcr.io/${{ github.repository }}/... and ghcr.io/nvidia-nemo/gym/...) only on non-PR push/tag events, a low blast radius. All of this supports keeping severity at low rather than upgrading. The finding stands as written; the devil neither overturns its facts nor its severity.
adjudication.final_recommendation: Keep as a low-severity, optional supply-chain hardening note, not a merge blocker. If adopted, set `provenance: mode=max` and `sbom: true` on the docker/build-push-action@v6 "Build and push" step in build-k8s-images.yml, and optionally add a cosign keyless signing step (id-token: write) for the pushed GHCR images. Note explicitly in the finding that this is consistent with the repo's existing _build_container.yml (which also lacks signing/SBOM/provenance), so it is parity-with-repo rather than a regression — which is why it remains low and non-blocking.
adjudication.evidence_examined: /tmp/pr-1231-worktree/.github/workflows/build-k8s-images.yml (lines 80-91 build-push step; no provenance/sbom/cosign keys); /tmp/pr-1231-worktree/.github/workflows/k8s-integration-test.yml (all builds load:true, no push, no signing); scoped grep for provenance|sbom|cosign|attest|sign across both files (exit 1, no matches); listing of .github/workflows/ confirming only these two workflows are new in the PR; /home/adasif/Desktop/Gym/.github/workflows/_build_container.yml (existing official image pipeline, grep for same tokens exit 1 — no repo signing convention to diverge from).

finding.title: No imagePullSecrets for private GHCR
finding.description: No pull secret; ImagePullBackOff if private. Compounds F28.
finding.evidence: no imagePullSecret in diff
finding.recommendation: Confirm public or add pull secret.

devil.challenge:
The finding is overstated and largely a non-issue, and the omission is a deliberate, defensible choice. Three concrete reasons:

1. The finding is self-defeatingly conditional. Its own recommendation is "Confirm public or add pull secret." All four images live under the official org namespace `ghcr.io/nvidia-nemo/gym/nemo-gym-{resources,agent,model,head}` (k8s/base/deployment-*.yaml, raycluster.yaml). For an open-source NVIDIA NeMo project publishing official images, public visibility is the obvious intent. If public (the expected case), there is NO problem and adding imagePullSecrets would be actively wrong — forcing users to create credentials they don't need. The finding only "breaks" in a hypothetical (private) configuration the project did not choose.

2. Pull secrets are inherently site/cluster-specific and do NOT belong in an upstream base. There is no Secret object to reference; hardcoding a `imagePullSecrets: [{name: ...}]` into k8s/base/serviceaccount.yaml would point at a non-existent secret and be a guessed name. The correct K8s pattern is to add the SA pull secret in the operator's own namespace or an overlay. The PR already uses overlays for environment-specific concerns (e.g. the openshift Component flips imagePullPolicy to Always). Leaving pull secrets to the operator is consistent, idiomatic, and intentional.

3. The demonstrated/CI deployment path is unaffected. k8s-integration-test.yml uses `kind load docker-image` (line 89) to inject images into the kind node, and base uses `imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent` everywhere — so the e2e test never performs a registry pull and never needs auth. The happy path is proven to work. There is no broken deploy, security hole, or data loss; severity "low" with duplicate_count 1 already reflects this.

The only residual truth is a minor README docs gap (k8s/README.md lines ~75-92 walk through deploy without mentioning private-registry pull secrets) — but that is a nit, not the deployment defect the finding frames it as.
devil.strongest_counterargument: The strongest reason the finding still stands: I cannot prove from the repo that the GHCR packages under ghcr.io/nvidia-nemo/gym/ are actually public — that depends on org/package settings outside the repo, and freshly pushed GHCR packages default to inheriting/needing explicit publicization (newly created packages are often private until made public). If they are private, every Deployment and the RayCluster worker would hit ImagePullBackOff on any non-CI cluster, since gym-sa has no imagePullSecrets and there's no per-Deployment imagePullSecrets either. Combined with the noted compounding F28, a first-time user following the README would get a stuck deploy with a non-obvious cause and no mention of pull secrets in the docs. So even if "low," it is a real, plausible failure mode plus a genuine documentation omission, which keeps the finding from being simply wrong.
points_to_verify: Actual GHCR visibility of ghcr.io/nvidia-nemo/gym/nemo-gym-resources|agent|model|head packages (org/package settings, not in repo) — public vs private decides whether this is a real failure or a non-issue
build-k8s-images.yml does not set or document package visibility (confirmed: only `packages: write` at line 51, no visibility step), so visibility is governed entirely outside the manifest set

k8s/base/serviceaccount.yaml has no imagePullSecrets (confirmed) and none of the Deployments/RayCluster pod specs set pod-level imagePullSecrets either (grep showed only image:/imagePullPolicy: lines)
k8s-integration-test.yml relies on `kind load docker-image` (line 89) + IfNotPresent, so CI never exercises a registry pull — confirm the e2e green status does NOT validate the pull path
Whether k8s/README.md documents adding a pull secret for private registries (grep of README showed no imagePullSecret/private guidance — appears to be a real docs gap)
Whether an overlay (openshift or composition overlays) injects imagePullSecrets anywhere — base SA does not, but confirm no overlay patch adds it

adjudication.reasoning:
Independently verified all six of the devil's points_to_verify.

CONFIRMED facts: (1) k8s/base/serviceaccount.yaml (full 9-line file) has NO imagePullSecrets. (2) A scoped grep over base/overlays/tests returned only image:/imagePullPolicy: lines — zero imagePullSecrets matches at SA OR pod level. (3) Rendering k8s/overlays/example-single-tool-call confirms every pod uses serviceAccountName: estc-gym-sa + imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent with no pull secret. (4) All images point at ghcr.io/nvidia-nemo/gym/nemo-gym-{head,model,agent,resources}:latest (deployment-*.yaml, raycluster.yaml at 4 sites). (5) The openshift Component ONLY patches imagePullPolicy to Always (containers/0 on deployment + RayCluster head/worker groups) — it injects no pull secret; in fact Always makes the private case worse since node-cached images get re-pulled every restart. (6) build-k8s-images.yml has packages: write (line 51) but NO package-visibility step — visibility is governed entirely outside the manifests; it pushes to ${{ github.repository }}/<suffix> (line 72), which only matches the hardcoded ghcr.io/nvidia-nemo/gym path if the canonical repo is exactly nvidia-nemo/gym. (7) k8s/README.md documents 'Using a Different Image Registry' via images: name override (lines 212-227) and claims pre-built GHCR images are available (line 77), but has zero private-registry/pull-secret/auth guidance — a genuine docs gap. CI (kind load + IfNotPresent) never exercises a registry pull, so green CI does NOT validate auth.

ADJUDICATION: The devil is correct that (a) hardcoding a guessed imagePullSecrets name into the upstream base would be actively wrong — there is no Secret object to reference; (b) pull secrets are legitimately a per-operator/overlay concern; (c) the demonstrated CI happy path works. However the devil's framing that the finding is 'overstated' is itself overstated: the finding's own recommendation is the correctly-hedged 'Confirm public or add pull secret', and it is already rated low with duplicate_count 1 — that is not overstatement. The strongest residual truth (which the devil concedes) holds: GHCR package visibility cannot be proven from the repo, freshly-pushed packages commonly default to private, and if private then every Deployment + RayCluster worker would ImagePullBackOff on any non-CI cluster with a non-obvious cause and no README guidance. So this is a real-but-conditional runtime risk plus a confirmed documentation omission. Neither 'confirmed' (the base-manifest framing implies a fix belongs in base, which is wrong) nor 'refuted' (the docs gap + unverifiable-visibility risk are real) fits — partially_confirmed is correct. Severity stays low: conditional on external package settings, base happy path proven, no security hole or data loss.
adjudication.final_recommendation: Do NOT add imagePullSecrets to k8s/base/serviceaccount.yaml — there is no Secret to reference and a guessed name would break clusters. Instead: (1) Treat this primarily as a docs fix — add a short 'Private registry' note to k8s/README.md near the existing 'Using a Different Image Registry' section (~lines 212-227) showing how an operator creates a dockerconfigjson Secret and patches it onto gym-sa via an overlay (kustomize patch adding imagePullSecrets to the ServiceAccount, which propagates to all pods). (2) Ensure the GHCR packages built by build-k8s-images.yml are actually published public (verify org/package settings, or add an explicit visibility step), since the manifests and README hardcode ghcr.io/nvidia-nemo/gym/... and assume public pull. (3) Optionally note that the openshift overlay's imagePullPolicy: Always amplifies this for private registries by re-pulling on every pod restart.

finding.title: No PodDisruptionBudget or availability primitives
finding.description: No PDB/PriorityClass/HPA; drain interrupts single-replica work. F32 is QoS.
finding.evidence: none in k8s
finding.recommendation: Add PDB and head PriorityClass.

devil.challenge:
The finding is partly factually wrong, partly out-of-scope, and its severity is correctly self-rated "low" but its substance does not survive scrutiny.

1) The "no HPA" complaint is simply incorrect and the recommendation would be harmful. k8s/base/raycluster.yaml lines 11-21 already enable Ray-native autoscaling: `enableInTreeAutoscaling: true` with `autoscalerOptions` and the gym-workers group set to `minReplicas: 1 / maxReplicas: 4` (lines 78-79). KubeRay's in-tree autoscaler is the idiomatic way to scale Ray worker pods based on pending Ray tasks; bolting a CPU-target HPA onto a RayCluster CRD would fight the Ray autoscaler over the same replica field and is an anti-pattern. So "no HPA" is not a gap — autoscaling is present, just done the correct (Ray) way.

2) The QoS premise is muddled and, where decipherable, describes a deliberate best-practice choice, not a defect. Across raycluster.yaml and the deployments, containers set CPU+memory requests and a memory limit but intentionally omit CPU limits (e.g. deployment-agent.yaml lines 68-73: `requests cpu 250m / memory 512Mi`, `limits: memory 1Gi`; resources group raycluster.yaml lines 190-195 same pattern). That yields Burstable QoS — the widely recommended pattern (memory limit to stop OOM noisy-neighbors, no CPU limit to avoid CFS throttling). Burstable is a defensible design, and in any case "QoS" is orthogonal to whether a PDB exists; the finding conflates two unrelated concerns.

3) The only substantive item — no PDB / drain interrupts single-replica work — is a genuine non-issue for THIS workload. k8s/README.md frames this subtree as an eval/benchmark harness (multiple benchmarks run to completion, asserting reward==1 in the kind e2e), not a high-availability online service. For replicas: 1 objects, a PDB cannot protect work: minAvailable:1 on a 1-replica Deployment blocks node drains entirely (wedging cluster maintenance), while maxUnavailable:1 is a no-op. PDBs deliver value for multi-replica services, which this is not. The PR already provides reasonable graceful-shutdown handling (preStop sleep, terminationGracePeriodSeconds 30-60, maxUnavailable:0 rolling updates) — the realistic availability levers for single-replica batch components are present.

4) The "head PriorityClass" recommendation is weak: the head is a stateless config-server (NEMO_GYM_CONFIG_DICT served over HTTP, deployment-head.yaml) that reschedules cleanly; PriorityClass only matters under node pressure/preemption, an HA concern out of scope for an optional eval harness explicitly judged "on its own merits."

Net: one of three named primitives (HPA) is already implemented the right way and the recommendation is counterproductive; the QoS framing is incoherent; and the PDB/PriorityClass asks are HA features that don't fit a single-replica batch eval harness. The author made defensible scoping choices.
devil.strongest_counterargument: Even granting all of the above, the finding self-rates "low" and is not strictly false in its narrowest form: there is literally no PodDisruptionBudget object anywhere under k8s/, and a voluntary node drain (kubectl drain during a cluster upgrade) WILL evict the single-replica gym-resources worker mid-task with only terminationGracePeriodSeconds (60s) of warning, losing in-flight rollout work that the resources server is executing. A maxUnavailable-style PDB plus checkpoint/retry would reduce that lost work. The drain-interruption mechanism described is real even if the chosen remedies are partly wrong. So the finding points at a genuine (if minor and arguably out-of-scope) operational characteristic, which is why I keep the probability-wrong below ~0.7 rather than calling it a clean false positive.
points_to_verify: Confirm raycluster.yaml lines 11-21 set enableInTreeAutoscaling:true with autoscalerOptions and gym-workers minReplicas:1/maxReplicas:4 (lines 78-79) — i.e. autoscaling IS present, so 'no HPA' is not a real gap and HPA would conflict with the Ray autoscaler.

Confirm CPU limits are omitted while memory limits are set in deployment-agent.yaml (68-73), deployment-head.yaml (59-64), deployment-model.yaml, and raycluster resources group (190-195) — i.e. QoS is intentionally Burstable, a best-practice choice, not BestEffort.
Confirm k8s/README.md positions this as an eval/benchmark harness (run-to-completion), not a production HA service — supporting that PDB/PriorityClass are out of scope.

Verify that a PDB on a replicas:1 Deployment cannot protect work (minAvailable:1 blocks drains; maxUnavailable:1 is a no-op) — i.e. the recommended remedy is ineffective for single-replica components.

Confirm graceful-shutdown primitives ALREADY present: preStop sleep hooks (deployment-agent.yaml 93-96, deployment-head.yaml 88-91), terminationGracePeriodSeconds (30/60), and maxUnavailable:0 rolling updates.

Decide whether the residual true core (no PDB; voluntary drain interrupts the single gym-resources worker mid-rollout) is severe enough to merit any action, given the harness's batch nature and the finding's own 'low' severity.

adjudication.reasoning:
I verified each of the devil's six points directly against the rendered/source manifests.

1) "No HPA" is factually FALSE. raycluster.yaml:11-21 sets `enableInTreeAutoscaling: true` with `autoscalerOptions` (upscalingMode Default, idleTimeoutSeconds 300, autoscaler resource requests/limits), and the `gym-workers` group at lines 76-79 has `minReplicas: 1 / maxReplicas: 4`. KubeRay's in-tree autoscaler is the idiomatic scaling mechanism for a RayCluster; a CPU-target HPA pointed at the same CRD replica field is an anti-pattern that fights the Ray autoscaler. (Caveat: the unit of work — the resources server — runs in the SEPARATE `gym-resources` worker group, pinned minReplicas:1/maxReplicas:1 at lines 122-125, so autoscaling does not scale the resources server. But that does not rescue the finding, which claims autoscaling is absent; it is present.)

2) The QoS premise ("F32 is QoS") is incoherent and, where decipherable, WRONG. Every workload sets requests plus a memory limit but intentionally omits a CPU limit: deployment-agent.yaml:68-73 (requests cpu 250m/mem 512Mi, limits memory 1Gi), deployment-head.yaml:59-64, deployment-model.yaml:68-73, and raycluster gym-resources:190-195. That is Burstable QoS (memory limit to bound OOM, no CPU limit to avoid CFS throttling) — a defensible best-practice pattern, not the BestEffort the finding implies. QoS is also orthogonal to whether a PDB exists.

3) README.md:3,26 positions this as a benchmark eval harness ("Deploy NeMo-Gym benchmarks"; data flow terminating in "reward (0.0 or 1.0)") — a run-to-completion batch system, not an HA online service. PDB/PriorityClass are HA primitives that do not fit this workload.

4) A PDB cannot protect work on these objects: all four workloads are single-replica (raycluster groups replicas:1; agent/model/head Deployments replicas:1). On a 1-replica target, minAvailable:1/maxUnavailable:0 BLOCKS voluntary drains entirely (wedges node maintenance), while maxUnavailable:1 is a no-op. The recommended remedy is therefore either harmful or useless for the actual topology.

5) Graceful-shutdown levers ARE present: preStop `sleep 5` (deployment-agent.yaml:93-96, deployment-head.yaml:88-91, plus model), terminationGracePeriodSeconds 30 (deployments) / 60 (raycluster pods), and maxUnavailable:0 rolling updates (agent/head/model :14-18).

6) Residual true kernel: there is no PodDisruptionBudget object anywhere under k8s/, and a voluntary `kubectl drain` will evict the single gym-resources worker mid-task with only 60s grace, losing in-flight rollout work. That mechanism is real. But it is minor, out of scope for an optional batch eval harness (re-runnable), and the finding's own proposed fixes are unsound on this topology.

Net: two of three named primitives (HPA, QoS) are factually mischaracterized; the third (PDB/PriorityClass) is technically-present-as-a-gap but the recommended remedy is ineffective/harmful for single-replica batch components. The devil's challenge substantially survives. I overturn the finding on substance.
adjudication.final_recommendation: Drop the HPA and QoS items entirely — autoscaling is already implemented correctly via KubeRay in-tree autoscaling (raycluster.yaml:11-21, gym-workers maxReplicas:4), and QoS is intentionally Burstable (memory limits set, CPU limits omitted across all workloads), which is best practice. Do not add a CPU-target HPA (it conflicts with the Ray autoscaler) and do not add a PDB to the single-replica Deployments/worker groups (minAvailable would block node drains; maxUnavailable would be a no-op). The only defensible residual note (nit-level): the single gym-resources worker (raycluster.yaml:122-125, pinned to 1 replica) loses in-flight rollout work on a voluntary node drain with only 60s grace. If operators care about that, the realistic mitigation is checkpoint/retry of rollout work or running the eval as a Job that retries — not a PDB. Given this is an optional, re-runnable batch eval harness (README.md:3,26) with preStop hooks, terminationGracePeriodSeconds, and maxUnavailable:0 already in place, no action is required for merge.

finding.title: No Ray metrics or ServiceMonitor
finding.description: Only README mentions metrics; 8265 unexposed. With F5, no operability.
finding.evidence: no ServiceMonitor in k8s
finding.recommendation: Add ServiceMonitor; structured logging.

devil.challenge: The finding is overstated and contains factual errors, though its narrow core (no ServiceMonitor object ships) is true. Specifically: (1) "8265 unexposed" is FALSE. k8s/base/raycluster.yaml lines 45-47 explicitly declare `containerPort: 8265, name: dashboard`, and line 24 sets `dashboard-host: "0.0.0.0"`. The Ray dashboard IS exposed at the container level, the KubeRay operator auto-creates the `*-gym-ray-head-svc`, and the README documents the exact access path twice (lines 164 and 375: `kubectl port-forward -n gym svc/cg-gym-ray-head-svc 8265:8265`). So the dashboard's rich operational UI (jobs, actors, resource usage, logs) is reachable. (2) "Only README mentions metrics" understates a DELIBERATE design decision: README line 342 explicitly states the resources server uses port 9080 specifically "to avoid conflict with Ray metrics on 8080," proving the author knew Ray auto-exposes Prometheus metrics on 8080 and architected around them. Ray metrics are emitted by the runtime itself, not something the PR must add. (3) Omitting a ServiceMonitor from a base kustomization is the CORRECT conservative default, not a defect: a ServiceMonitor depends on the Prometheus Operator CRD (`monitoring.coreos.com/v1`); bundling one into a base that every cluster applies would make `kubectl apply` FAIL on any cluster lacking that operator. Scrape-target selection is a cluster-operator concern, deliberately left to the deployer. (4) The "no operability" conclusion is explicitly chained to finding F5 ("With F5, no operability") — it is conditional, not standalone, and `kubectl exec ... cat /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log` (README line 363) plus the Ray dashboard give a documented operability story. (5) Severity is already correctly self-rated "low," and the structured-logging recommendation is a separate scope-creep suggestion, not a manifest defect. Net: the finding's evidence string ("8265 unexposed") is provably wrong, and its framing as an operability gap overlooks the exposed-and-documented dashboard plus the intentional metrics-port design.
devil.strongest_counterargument: Despite the factual errors in the evidence string, the finding's CORE assertion survives: the PR ships no `ServiceMonitor` and adds no Prometheus scrape annotations (`prometheus.io/scrape`) to the resources/agent/model pods, so Ray's metrics on 8080 and the FastAPI servers are not actually scraped by any monitoring stack out of the box. The Ray dashboard is interactive-only (not a substitute for time-series metrics/alerting), and `kubectl exec ... cat /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log` is unstructured plaintext logging on an emptyDir that is lost on pod restart with no aggregation. For a PR whose README claims production-grade "mTLS-authenticated distributed task execution," the absence of any metrics-export path is a legitimate (low-severity) observability gap. The finding correctly identifies that gap and is appropriately rated low — the inaccurate detail ('8265 unexposed') is a flaw in the supporting evidence, not a reason to dismiss the underlying issue.
points_to_verify: Confirm k8s/base/raycluster.yaml lines 45-47 declare containerPort 8265 named 'dashboard' and line 24 sets dashboard-host 0.0.0.0 — verifying the 'unexposed' claim is wrong at the container level
Confirm the KubeRay operator (not the PR) auto-generates the gym-ray-head-svc that the README port-forward command targets, so dashboard access genuinely works

Verify NO pod in k8s/base/ (raycluster.yaml, deployment-agent.yaml, deployment-model.yaml, deployment-head.yaml) carries prometheus.io/scrape annotations or any ServiceMonitor — the true core of the finding
Verify README line 342 ('avoid conflict with Ray metrics on 8080') to confirm the author was aware of and designed around Ray's auto-exposed metrics
Check whether a ServiceMonitor in the base kustomization would break apply on clusters lacking the Prometheus Operator CRD, supporting the 'intentional omission' rebuttal
Confirm the finding text 'With F5, no operability' makes the conclusion conditional on a separate finding rather than standalone
Confirm server logs go only to /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log on an emptyDir (raycluster.yaml line 156) with no aggregation, which is the genuine residual logging weakness

adjudication.reasoning: Core assertion is TRUE: no ServiceMonitor ships and no Prometheus scrape path is wired. A scoped grep across all of k8s returned zero matches for prometheus, servicemonitor, or scrape, and the deployment-agent and raycluster pod templates carry only labels with no prometheus.io scrape annotations. But the evidence string is wrong: it claims 8265 is unexposed, while raycluster.yaml line 24 sets dashboard-host 0.0.0.0 and lines 45-47 declare containerPort 8265 named dashboard, and README lines 164 and 375 document kubectl port-forward against the KubeRay-generated head service, so the dashboard is exposed and reachable. The no-operability framing is overstated and explicitly conditional on finding F5. The devil is right that omitting a ServiceMonitor from base is a defensible default since a monitoring.coreos.com/v1 object would make kubectl apply fail on clusters without the Prometheus Operator CRD. Residual gaps survive: no out-of-box metrics scraping, and logs go only to /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log on an emptyDir, unstructured and lost on pod restart. Real but minor, so the finding survives at low severity with corrected evidence.
adjudication.final_recommendation: Keep at LOW but rewrite the evidence: the claim that 8265 is unexposed is factually wrong since raycluster.yaml lines 24 and 45-47 expose the Ray dashboard and README lines 164 and 375 document port-forward access via the KubeRay-generated cg-gym-ray-head-svc. Genuine residual gaps: no Prometheus scrape path out of the box (no ServiceMonitor and no prometheus.io/scrape annotations anywhere in k8s/), and server logs go only to /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log on an emptyDir (raycluster.yaml lines 156 and 224-227), unstructured and lost on pod restart. Optional non-blocking fix: ship a ServiceMonitor as a separate Kustomize Component or overlay (not base, to avoid breaking apply on clusters without the Prometheus Operator CRD) plus scrape annotations, and optionally log to stdout for aggregation. Not a deploy blocker.
adjudication.evidence_examined: Read raycluster.yaml (full 228 lines), deployment-agent.yaml (full), README lines 160-171 and 340-375. Scoped grep over /tmp/pr-1231-worktree/k8s/ for prometheus, servicemonitor, scrape, metrics found no prometheus/servicemonitor/scrape; only metrics hit is README line 342. Confirmed raycluster.yaml line 24 dashboard-host 0.0.0.0, lines 45-47 containerPort 8265 named dashboard, line 156 log to /tmp/nemo-gym-server.log, lines 224-227 /tmp emptyDir. README lines 164 and 375 dashboard port-forward, line 342 avoid conflict with Ray metrics on 8080, line 363 exec cat log.
